{
  "markdown": "Spider: Yale Semantic Parsing and Text-to-SQL Challenge\n\n# Spider 1.0\n\n## Yale Semantic Parsing and Text-to-SQL Challenge\n\n## What is Spider?\n\nNov 12, 2024: We have released [Spider 2.0](https://spider2-sql.github.io/) full [paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2411.07763), [data](https://github.com/xlang-ai/Spider2/blob/main/spider2/README.md) and [code](https://github.com/xlang-ai/Spider2). Follow the [guideline](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1sCobAqJZcko-Vl3biOycwvCIR7kTwBPrhsgVfvaX1Fg/edit?tab=t.0) to submit your scores to the [leaderboard](https://spider2-sql.github.io/)!\n\nAug 28, 2024: The early access version of [Spider 2.0](https://spider2-sql.github.io/)(a more realistic and challenging text-to-SQL task) is now available! We expect to release the whole dataset in 1-2 weeks. As this is a preliminary release, there may be errors. Your feedback would be invaluable in refining the dataset!\n\nSpider is a large-scale [complex and cross-domain](https://medium.com/@tao.yu/spider-one-more-step-towards-natural-language-interfaces-to-databases-62298dc6df3c) semantic parsing and text-to-SQL dataset annotated by 11 Yale students. The goal of the Spider challenge is to develop natural language interfaces to cross-domain databases. It consists of 10,181 questions and 5,693 unique complex SQL queries on 200 databases with multiple tables covering 138 different domains. In Spider 1.0, different complex SQL queries and databases appear in train and test sets. To do well on it, systems must generalize well to not only new SQL queries but also new database schemas.\n\n---\n\nWhy we call it \"Spider\"? It is because our dataset is complex and cross-domain like a spider crawling across mutiple complex(with many foreign keys) nests(databases). [XLANG Lab for Building LLM/VLM Agents](https://www.xlang.ai/) [Spider Paper (EMNLP'18)](https://arxiv.org/abs/1809.08887) [Spider Post](https://medium.com/@tao.yu/spider-one-more-step-towards-natural-language-interfaces-to-databases-62298dc6df3c)\n\n---\n\nRelated Works from XLANG Lab: [Spider 2.0 Text-to-SQL (ICLR 25)](https://spider2-sql.github.io/) [Spider2-V (NeurIPS 24)](https://spider2-v.github.io/) [OSWorld (NeurIPS 24)](https://os-world.github.io/) [DS-1000 Challenge (ICML'23)](https://ds1000-code-gen.github.io/) [Binder Framework (ICLR '23)](https://lm-code-binder.github.io/) [UnifiedSKG Framework (EMNLP'22)](https://github.com/hkunlp/unifiedskg) [SParC Challenge (ACL'19)](https://yale-lily.github.io/sparc) [CoSQL Challenge (EMNLP'19)](https://yale-lily.github.io/cosql)\n\n## News\n\n- 11/12/2024 We have released [Spider 2.0](https://spider2-sql.github.io/) full [paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2411.07763), [data](https://github.com/xlang-ai/Spider2/blob/main/spider2/README.md) and [code](https://github.com/xlang-ai/Spider2). Follow the [guideline](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1sCobAqJZcko-Vl3biOycwvCIR7kTwBPrhsgVfvaX1Fg/edit?tab=t.0) to submit your scores to the [leaderboard](https://spider2-sql.github.io/).\n- 08/28/2024 The early access version of [Spider 2.0](https://spider2-sql.github.io/)(a more realistic and challenging text-to-SQL task) is now available! As this is a preliminary release, there may be errors. Your feedback would be invaluable in refining the dataset!\n- 07/15/2024 [Spider 2.0-vision (Benchmarking Multimodal Agents on Automating Data Science and Engineering Workflows)](https://spider2-v.github.io/) is out! Spider 2.0-SQL (much more realistic and challenging than Spider 1.0!) will be released in August.\n- 02/05/2024 We will no longer accept submissions for Spider 1.0 evaluations or update its leaderboard. The test set of Spider 1.0 has already been released (check the Spider dataset link below). Look forward to the release of Spider 2.0, a more realistic and challenging benchmark in the era of LLMs, expected this March June. Stay tuned!\n- 08/10/2023 Please check out [XLANG Lab for Building LLM/VLM Agents](https://xlang.ai/)!\n- 05/27/2023 Please check out [Dr.Spider, a robustness evaluation benchmark based on Spider,](https://openreview.net/pdf?id=Wc5bmZZU9cy) from AWS AI Lab for studying robustness in semantic parsing!\n- 11/20/2022 Please check out our recent work [DS-1000: A Natural and Reliable Benchmark for Data Science Code Generation](https://ds1000-code-gen.github.io/). Please check out examples, data, and code on the [DS-1000 project site](https://ds1000-code-gen.github.io/)!!\n- 10/18/2022 Please check out our recent work [Binder: an easy but sota neural-symbolic built on GPT-3 Codex \u0026 SQL/Python interpreter](https://lm-code-binder.github.io/). It injects GPT-3 Codex prompt API calls in programming languages! Please check out Binder demo, code, paper, and video on the [Binder project site](https://yale-lily.github.io/[https://github.com/hkunlp/unifiedskg](https://lm-code-binder.github.io/))!!\n- 01/18/2022 Please check out our recent work [UnifiedSKG: Unifying and Multi-Tasking Structured Knowledge Grounding with Text-to-Text Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2201.05966). We open-sourced simple but SOTA/strong models for 21 tasks including text-to-SQL! Please check out our code in the [UnifiedSKG repo](https://github.com/hkunlp/unifiedskg)!!\n- 03/11/2021 Please check out [a nice work](https://arxiv.org/abs/2010.12725) from Google Research (including [new Spider splits](https://github.com/google-research/language/tree/master/language/nqg)) for studying compositional generalization in semantic parsing!\n- 11/15/2020 We will use [Test Suite Accuracy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2010.02840) as our official evaluation metric for Spider, SParC, and CoSQL. Please find the evaluation code from [here](https://github.com/taoyds/test-suite-sql-eval). Also, Notice that Test results after May 02, 2020 are reported on the new release (collected some annotation errors).\n- 08/03/2020 Corrected \"column_name\" and \"column_name_original\" mismatches in 2 dbs (\"scholar\" and \"formula_1\") in tables.json, and reparsed SQL queries (this only affects some models (e.g. RATSQL) which use our parsed SQL as the SQL input). Please download the Spider dataset from this page again.\n- 06/07/2020 We corrected some annotation errors and label mismatches (not errors) in Spider dev and test sets (~4% of dev examples updated, click [here](https://github.com/taoyds/spider/commit/25fcd85d9b6e94acaeb5e9172deadeefeed83f5e#diff-18b0a730a7b0d29b0a78a5070d971d49) for more details). Please download the Spider dataset from this page again.\n- 01/16/2020 For value prediction (in order to compute the execution accuracy), your model should be able to 1) copy from the question inputs, 2) retrieve from the database content (database content is available), or 3) generate numbers (e.g. 3 in \"LIMIT 3\").\n- 9/24/2019 [(Min et al., EMNLP 2019)](https://frcchang.github.io/pub/emnlp2019.2.pdf) translated Spider to Chinese! Check out [the Chinese challenge page](https://taolusi.github.io/CSpider-explorer/).\n- 5/17/2019 Our paper [SParC: Cross-Domain Semantic Parsing in Context](https://arxiv.org/abs/1906.02285) with Salesforce Research was accepted to ACL 2019! It introduces the context-dependent version of the Spider challenge: [SParC](https://yale-lily.github.io/sparc)!\n- 5/17/2019 Please report any [annotation errors here](https://github.com/taoyds/spider/issues/24), we really appreciate your help and will update the data release in this summer!\n- 1/14/2019 The [submission tutorial](https://worksheets.codalab.org/worksheets/0x82150f426cb94c17b861ef4162817399/) is out!.\n- 12/17/2018 We updated 7 sqlite database files ([issue 14](https://github.com/taoyds/spider/issues/14)). Please download the Spider dataset from this page again.\n- 10/25/2018 The evaluation script and results were updated ([issue 5](https://github.com/taoyds/spider/issues/5)). Please download the lastest versions of the script and papers. Also, please follow instructions in [issue 3](https://github.com/taoyds/spider/issues/3) to generate the latest SQL parsing results (fixed a bug).\n\n## Why Spider?\n\nAs the above spider chart shows, Spider 1.0 is distinct from most of the previous semantic parsing tasks because:\n\n- [ATIS, Geo, Academic](https://github.com/jkkummerfeld/text2sql-data): Each of them contains only a single database with a limited number of SQL queries, and has exact same SQL queries in train and test splits.\n- [WikiSQL](https://github.com/salesforce/WikiSQL): The numbers of SQL queries and tables are significantly large. But all SQL queries are simple, and each database is only a simple table without any foreign key.\n\nSpider 1.0 spans the largest area in the chart, making it the first complex and cross-domain semantic parsing and text-to-SQL dataset! Read more on [the blog post](https://medium.com/@tao.yu/spider-one-more-step-towards-natural-language-interfaces-to-databases-62298dc6df3c).\n\n## Getting Started\n\nThe data is split into training, development, and test sets. Download a copy of the dataset (distributed under the [CC BY-SA 4.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/legalcode) license):\n\n[Spider Dataset](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1403EGqzIDoHMdQF4c9Bkyl7dZLZ5Wt6J/view?usp=sharing) Details of baseline models and evaluation script can be found on the following GitHub site: [Spider GitHub Page](https://github.com/taoyds/spider)\n\n## Data Examples\n\nSome examples look like the following:\n\n## Have Questions or Want to Contribute ?\n\nAsk us questions at our [Github issues page](https://github.com/taoyds/spider/issues) or contact [Tao Yu](https://taoyds.github.io/), [Rui Zhang](https://ryanzhumich.github.io/), or [Michihiro Yasunaga](https://michiyasunaga.github.io/).\n\nWe expect the dataset to evolve. We would greatly appreciate it if you could donate us your non-private databases or SQL queries for the project.\n\n## Acknowledgement\n\nWe thank [Graham Neubig](http://www.phontron.com/), [Tianze Shi](http://www.cs.cornell.edu/~tianze/), [Catherine Finegan-Dollak](http://www-personal.umich.edu/~cfdollak/), and the anonymous reviewers for their precious comments on this project. Also, we thank [Pranav Rajpurkar](https://rajpurkar.github.io/) for giving us the permission to build this website based on [SQuAD](https://rajpurkar.github.io/SQuAD-explorer/).\n\nOur team at the summit of the East Rock park in New Haven (The pose is \"NLseq2SQL\"):\n\n## Leaderboard - Execution with Values\n\nOur current models do not predict any value in SQL conditions so that we do not provide execution accuracies. However, we encourage you to provide it in the future submissions. For value prediction, your model should be able to 1) copy from the question inputs, 2) retrieve from the database content (database content is available), or 3) generate numbers (e.g. 3 in \"LIMIT 3\"). {
  "markdown": "BIRD-bench\n\n#### About BIRD\n\nBIRD (BIg Bench for LaRge-scale Database Grounded Text-to-SQL Evaluation) represents a pioneering, cross-domain dataset that examines the impact of extensive database contents on text-to-SQL parsing. BIRD contains over 12,751 unique question-SQL pairs, 95 big databases with a total size of 33.4 GB. It also covers more than 37 professional domains, such as blockchain, hockey, healthcare and education, etc.\n\n#### News\n\n- 🚀 Mar. 23, 2026: We release [BIRD-Critic-SQLite](https://huggingface.co/datasets/birdsql/bird-critic-1.0-sqlite), a new single-dialect benchmark containing 500 high-quality user issues focused on real-world SQLite database applications. Along with the dataset, we also release 3 RL-trained models: [BIRD-Talon-14B](https://huggingface.co/birdsql/BIRD-Talon-14b), [BIRD-Talon-7B](https://huggingface.co/birdsql/BIRD-Talon-7b), and [BIRD-Zeno-7B](https://huggingface.co/birdsql/BIRD-Zeno-7b). Data is available on [HuggingFace](https://huggingface.co/datasets/birdsql/bird-critic-1.0-sqlite) and evaluation script on [GitHub](https://github.com/bird-bench/BIRD-CRITIC-1).\n- Mar. 3, 2026: [LiveSQLBench-Large-v1](https://huggingface.co/datasets/birdsql/livesqlbench-large-v1) is released! The industrial-scale counterpart featuring ~1K columns, ~54 tables each DB with 480 tasks, 10x schema complexity over LiveSQLBench-Base-Full-v1, ~84K avg prompt tokens for long-context challenge, and Business Rule Drift (external knowledge can change and even has inconsistency across releases) for live context-learning evaluation. See more at [livesqlbench.ai](https://livesqlbench.ai/).\n- Mar. 6, 2026: We release the [Data Intelligence Index](https://livesqlbench.ai/data-intelligence-index/), a comprehensive evaluation of frontier AI models and agents on data-centric intelligence, covering DB querying, BI analysis, DB application debugging, human-centric interaction, multi-modal querying, and more. If you'd like your benchmark or model to be included, please feel free to [contact us](mailto:bird.bench25@gmail.com)!\n- 🇧🇷 Feb. 8, 2026: Our paper [BIRD-Interact](https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.05318) has been accepted to ICLR 2026 (Oral)! See you in Rio 🇧🇷！\n- 🌴 Nov. 30, 2025: Our paper [SWE-SQL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.18951) has been accepted to NeurIPS 2025 (main track)! You can find us in San Diego 🌴 🦞. Feel free to contact us if you’d like to chat: [Patrick](mailto:jl0725@connect.hku.hk), [Xiaolong Li](mailto:xia01ong@connect.hku.hk).\n- Nov. 13, 2025: Major Updates 1. Our team with the efforts of global engineers, experts, and students has completed comprehensive quality control for BIRD-SQL, releasing [bird-sql-dev-1106](https://huggingface.co/datasets/birdsql/bird_sql_dev_20251106), which is a cleaner development split. For new submissions using this updated split, please indicate this in your submission so we can mark it accordingly on the leaderboard. To better resolve ambiguity, we will open a new track of interactive setting as we did in BIRD-Interact and remove Oracle Evidence instead. Coming soon! 2. We have released [Mini-Interact](https://huggingface.co/datasets/birdsql/mini-interact), an SQLite-based interactive benchmark with a self-contained environment that requires no Docker setup. This lightweight version enables fast development and testing of interactive intelligence for agentic data exploration with users.\n- Oct. 9, 2025: We release our paper of [BIRD-Interact](https://huggingface.co/papers/2510.05318), detailing how we built the dataset and our key findings on why interaction and communication are critical for frontier LLMs to become more reliable assistance in the DBA cycle. Currently, GPT-5 (Med) only achieves 8.67% SR on&#96;c-Interact&#96; and 17.00% SR on&#96;a-Interact&#96; of full tasks. Additionally, we will release&#96;Mini-Interact&#96; which will be operated in sqlite, with our trained local and stable user simulator. It is currently undergoing alignment and function testing before release. If you think our work is helpful to you, we will feel grateful if you can star us, which is a strong motivation for boosting our open-source efforts!\n- Sep. 19, 2025: We’ve released [BIRD23-train-filtered](https://huggingface.co/datasets/birdsql/bird23-train-filtered), a high-quality filtered subset of the BIRD train split (6,601/9,428 ≈70%) that works as a drop-in replacement for text-to-SQL finetuning; See the example finetuning usage in [here](https://github.com/bird-bench/mini_dev/tree/main/finetuning).\n- Sep. 10, 2025: We're excited to announce the release of the [LiveSQLBench-Base-Full-V1 (600)](https://huggingface.co/datasets/birdsql/livesqlbench-base-full-v1)! The first text-to-SQL benchmark covering all SQL spectrum with Hierarchical Knowlegde Base (HKB) and test cases. We provide two types of queries: normal query and colloquial queries for people to test according to their own needs. The flag model Gemini-2.5-pro can only achieve 28.67 in colloquial queries, and 35.67 in normal queries. The [base-lite](https://huggingface.co/datasets/birdsql/livesqlbench-base-lite) and [base-full-v1](https://huggingface.co/datasets/birdsql/livesqlbench-base-full-v1) would be locked version for development of research methods. The detailed performance is in our [website](https://livesqlbench.ai/).\n- Aug. 26, 2025: We're excited to announce the release of the [BIRD-Interact-Full (600)](https://huggingface.co/datasets/birdsql/bird-interact-full) set! It's a tough one—the best LLMs are only achieving a 16.33% success rate, with just 10.0% on the&#96;c-interact&#96; and&#96;a-interact&#96; portions. For more details, please visit our [project website](https://bird-interact.github.io/). We'll be sending the Ground Truth \u0026 Test cases to our mailing list this week. If you want early access, please send an email as instructed on the site for an automatic download. On another note, we've also released a SQLite version of [LiveSQLBench-Lite](https://huggingface.co/datasets/birdsql/livesqlbench-base-lite-sqlite) for easier local research. The full LiveSQLBench-Base and -Large versions are coming soon!\n- Jul. 15, 2025: LLMs always struggle with self-correction due to self-enhancement bias, a problem particularly pronounced in SQL generation where declarative syntax and concise logs would provide limited guidance. Our new work in&#96;ACL Main&#96;, SHARE, mitigates this significantly by (1) converting SQL queries into procedural programming steps and (2) proposing a generalized pipeline through on-policy multi-agent methods for fine-tuning SLMs. Models: [BAM](https://huggingface.co/birdsql/share-bam), [SAM](https://huggingface.co/birdsql/share-sam), [LOM](https://huggingface.co/birdsql/share-lom) Dataset: [https://huggingface.co/datasets/birdsql/share-bam](https://huggingface.co/datasets/birdsql/share-bam) Paper: [https://huggingface.co/papers/2506.00391](https://huggingface.co/papers/2506.00391) Code: [https://github.com/quge2023/SHARE](https://github.com/quge2023/SHARE)\n- Jul. 10, 2025: We optimized and upload [bird-mini-dev](https://huggingface.co/datasets/birdsql/bird_mini_dev) with 3 dialects to huggingface according to community. Thanks!! Please download and check!\n- Jul. 10, 2025: We release the human performance scores on [BIRD-CRITIC 1.0](https://bird-critic.github.io/)! It contains both the group with and without usage of GenAI Tools. The huge gap encourages more intelligent solutions to serve users in the real-world DB applications. Please check~~.\n- Jun. 25, 2025: We document our procedures, findings, and training recipe for open-source LLM agents that resolve SQL debugging tasks in our paper [SWE-SQL](https://huggingface.co/papers/2506.18951).\n- Jun. 08, 2025: We have released bird-critic-1.0-postgresql (sql issues for single dialects). Check out the data in [Hugging Face](https://huggingface.co/datasets/birdsql/bird-critic-1.0-postgresql) and the newest code in [GitHub](https://github.com/bird-bench/BIRD-CRITIC-1). It seems that bird-critic is a challenging reasoning tasks for text-to-SQL since all top-peforming models are reasoning-based models. Have fun! Thanks!\n- Jun 4, 2025: We release [BIRD-Interact](https://bird-interact.github.io/), a comprehensive interactive evaluation for text-to-SQL models. It contains conversational (&#96;c-Interact&#96;) and agentic (&#96;a-Interact&#96;) interaction modes. Top results: o3-mini achieves 24.4% Success Rate on&#96;c-Interact&#96;, while Claude-3.7-Sonnet reaches 17.78% Success Rate on&#96;a-Interact&#96;. We also discovered Interaction-Time Scaling (ITS) - performance scales over extended interactions. Enjoy!\n- May 29, 2025: We release [LiveSQLBench](https://livesqlbench.ai/), the first containmination-free text-to-SQL benchmark covering full SQL specturm featuring more advanced SQLs, annotated RDBs, structrued / unstructured knowledge base, test cases, etc. First, we present&#96;LiveSQLBench-Base-Lite&#96; with 270 tasks for trail. Even though Lite is the easiest version with the most clear conditions, the SOTA model&#96;o3-mini&#96; only achieves 44.81% Success Rate. We will update full set and settings. You can preview and interact with data samples in our website. Stay tuned!\n- May. 25, 2025: Please report issues when you find any more of BIRD-SQL 2023, we will check and clean dev set (1534) for the last time during summer by considering all feedbacks. Next week, we will start our new project&#96;LiveSQLBench&#96;, the first one-stop containmination-free text-to-SQL benchmark covering full SQL specturm featuring more advanced SQLs, databases, hierarichal / unstructured knoweldge base, test cases. Also it supports multi-turn conversational and interactive evaluation via our&#96;BIRD-Interact&#96;, which will be released together.\n- May 22, 2025: We update Single-Model Leaderboard, now the Self-Consistency column has 4 values: empty (no self consistency used); Few: 1-7 candidates used in majority vote; Many: 8-32 candidates; Scale: \u003e32 candidates;\n- Apr. 20, 2025: We have released bird-critic-1.0-open (600 tasks by 4 dialects). Check out the data in [Hugging Face](https://huggingface.co/datasets/birdsql/bird-critic-1.0-open) and the newest code in [GitHub](https://github.com/bird-bench/BIRD-CRITIC-1). The full set of PostgreSQL will be released 1 week later. It seems that bird-critic is a challenging reasoning tasks for text-to-SQL since all top-peforming models are reasoning-based models. Have fun! Thanks!\n- Feb. 4, 2025: We've launched [BIRD-Critic (a.k.a SWE-SQL)](https://bird-critic.github.io/), a brand new text-to-SQL benchmark that really digs into reasoning challenges! A lite version is ready for exploration. Full sets are coming soon! Feel free for any feedbacks! Your inputs and suggestions are much appreciated!\n- Nov. 26, 2024: Thanks the support of&#96;BIRD-SQL 2023&#96;! Now we are pleased to share that the project&#96;BIRD 2025&#96; has been started. It will contains 4-6 new benchmarks with each covering its special focus of professional databases and their knowledge in the wild applications. We will release the first benchmark by early Jan. Feel free to let us know your needs or suggestions for cooking new generations of Text-to-SQL challenges. Thanks!\n- Aug. 4, 2024: The Reward-based Valid Efficiency Score (R-VES) will be used as the efficiency metric for future test submissions. The rationale and formula for R-VES can be found in the [Mini-Dev repository](https://github.com/bird-bench/mini_dev). You can check the legacy VES scores for previous submissions [here](https://abundant-dawn-033.notion.site/BIRD-VES-Leaderboard-Legacy-7aea622573dd42c59a1bcdb122d2cf44?pvs=4).\n- Jun. 30, 2024: Due to large requests of test submissions about mixed models (open-source + GPU-based closed source), we update the submission instructions to accelerate your waiting time. Please check it out!\n- Jun. 30, 2024: If you are interested in code agent, please do not miss a SOTA code agent implementation by [OpenDevin](https://github.com/All-Hands-AI/OpenHands/tree/main/evaluation/bird) for BIRD Dev!\n- Jun. 27, 2024: Excited to announce the release of our BIRD Mini-Dev dataset with 500 high-quality examples. This dataset includes all BIRD keywords, with modifications for questions such as the addition of&#96;window function&#96;. We are the first to deliver it in not only SQLite, but also MySQL, and PostgreSQL. We include Soft-F1 and R-VES metrics to reduce bias. Don't miss the&#96;column_meaning.json&#96; file, preprocessed by TA-SQL. Available for dev and testing set. Check out our work here: [Before Generation, Align it! A Novel and Effective Strategy for Mitigating Hallucinations in Text-to-SQL Generation (TA-SQL)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.15307), appearing at&#96;ACL 2024&#96; Findings.\n- Apr. 27, 2024: Due to large volume of requests, we now modify the license of our data to&#96;CC BY-SA 4.0&#96;. However, we will not take responsibility of any bad purposes by using our data. Since we develop this benchmark for research and healthy application only.\n- Mar 13, 2024: Please also take a look at our related work: [Tapilot-Crossing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2403.05307), which is the first challenging and more realistic benchmark designed to evaluate Large Language Model (LLM) agents on interactive data analysis tasks. The code includes Python and Private Library. And it covers 6 common agent actions in evaluation.\n- Sept 25, 2023: We have released a cleaner version of&#96;dev set&#96;. Please download dev set again. We checked all cases of dev set and fixed all errors that we found. After cleaning, the ChatGPT (gpt-3.5-turbo) and GPT4 (gpt-4-32k) EX scores have improved to 42.24 (from 37.22) and 49.15 (from 46.35), respectively. Thanks for all feedbacks!\n- Sept 21, 2023: Our paper has been accepted by&#96;NeurIPS 2023&#96; as a&#96;Spotlight&#96;!!! Thanks for all the efforts and suggestions of co-authors, anonymous reviewers, awesome researchers/users in github or emails.\n- July 17, 2023: We update newest results of GPT-4, Claude-2 and Palm-2.\n- July 14, 2023: The data link has been updated, fixing the schema names in the CSV files. Additionally, tied results caused by&#96;order_by limit 1&#96; are now considered. Both SQL queries - with and without accounting for tied results - are valid at this time.\n- Jun 12, 2023: We are welcome to any suggestions and reported gold errors in [help_wanted](https://github.com/AlibabaResearch/DAMO-ConvAI/issues/39). Any of your help is appreciated!\n- Jun 5, 2023: We open-sourced our [Graphix-T5](https://arxiv.org/abs/2301.07507), a graph-aware semi-pretrained text-to-text PLM specifically designed to improve multi-hop reasoning for the complex text-to-SQL task.\n- May 30, 2023: If you are interested in ICL, please check out our interesting work [deep-thinking🤔.](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2305.13016.pdf) Generate 1000 models for 1000 people smoothly!\n\n#### Surprise from BIRD\n\n1. Large and Dirty values: Due to the nature of the real-world scenarios from which BIRD's database values were collected, they typically retain their original and frequently \"dirty\" format. Hence, text-to-SQL parsers must first analyze these values to account for their non-standard format before engaging in reasoning.\n\n2. External Knowledge: \"account.type = 'OWNER'\" can be inferred by the knowledge evidence: \"The condition of the loans require the account type should be the owner.\"\n\n3. Text-to-Efficient-SQL: BIRD is the first text-to-SQL benchmark designed to encourage semantic parsers to produce SQL queries that are not only correct but also efficient. This emphasis on efficiency is especially valuable in real-world data / business analysis circumstances.\n\n#### Submission\n\nPlease follow the Submission Guideline (below) and contact&#96;bird.bench23@gmail.com&#96; for test evaluation. Ususally, we will return your results in 10 days!\n\n#### Subscribe to BIRD Update\n\nBird is a long-term research project aimed at bridging the gap between semantic parsing models and the success of database applications. For more details, please visit the [GitHub repository](https://github.com/bird-bench/mini_dev). For updating Leaderboard, please make sure your paper or resource is public available and submit a PR.\n\nMini Dev - Execution Accuracy (EX)\n\n| Model | Code | Size | Oracle Knowledge | SQLite | MySQL | PostgreSQL |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| Jun 31, 2024 | TA + GPT-4 HKU [[Qu et al. Self-consistency is allowed as it reflects the model's own capabilities, and we categorize submissions based on the number of candidates used. The Self-Consistency column indicates: empty (Self-Consistency is not used); Few (1-7 candidates); Many (8-32 candidates); Scale (\u003e32 candidates).\n\nLeaderboard - Execution Accuracy (EX)\n\n| Model | Code | Size | Self Consistency | Dev (%) | Test (%) |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| Human Performance Data Engineers + DB Students | 92.96 |\n| Mar 12, 2026 | Gemini-SQL (Multitask SFT + Gemini-2.5-Pro) Google Cloud Databases [Google Cloud Databases](https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/databases/how-to-get-gemini-to-deeply-understand-your-database) | UNK | Many | 73.27 | 77.14 |\n| Dec 4, 2025 | Q-SQL AWS-Quick Science [[Ravi Shankar et al.'25]](https://www.notion.so/BIRD-v2-Memory-2bfe8bf30514801ba112f92ff20784e3) | 30B-3B-MoE | Many | 72.99 | 76.47 |\n| July 08, 2025 | Databricks RLVR 32B Databricks Mosaic Research [Databricks Mosaic Research](https://www.databricks.com/research) | 32B | Few | - {
  "markdown": "Can LLM Already Serve as A Database Interface?\nA BIg Bench for Large-Scale Database Grounded\nText-to-SQLs\nJinyang Li1,♣ ‡, Binyuan Hui2,♣, Ge Qu1,♣, Binhua Li2, Jiaxi Yang2, Bowen Li3, Bailin Wang4,\nBowen Qin2, Rongyu Cao2, Ruiying Geng2, Nan Huo1, Xuanhe Zhou3, Chenhao Ma5,\nGuoliang Li3, Kevin C.C. Chang6 †, Fei Huang2, Reynold Cheng1 †, Yongbin Li2 †\n1 The University of Hong Kong 2 DAMO Academy, Alibaba Group\n3 Tsinghua University\n4 Massachusetts Institute of Technology\n5 The Chinese University of Hong Kong (Shenzhen)\n6 University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign\njl0725@connect.hku.hk, ckcheng@cs.hku.hk\nbinyuan.hby@alibaba-inc.com\nAbstract\nText-to-SQL parsing, which aims at converting natural language instructions into\nexecutable SQLs, has gained increasing attention in recent years. In particular,\nCodex and ChatGPT have shown impressive results in this task. However, most of\nthe prevalent benchmarks, i.e., Spider, and WikiSQL, focus on database schema\nwith few rows of database contents leaving the gap between academic study and\nreal-world applications. To mitigate this gap, we present BIRD, a BIg benchmark\nfor laRge-scale Database grounded in text-to-SQL tasks, containing 12,751 pairs\nof text-to-SQL data and 95 databases with a total size of 33.4 GB, spanning\n37 professional domains. Our emphasis on database values highlights the new\nchallenges of dirty database contents, external knowledge between NL questions\nand database contents, and SQL efficiency, particularly in the context of massive\ndatabases. To solve these problems, text-to-SQL models must feature database\nvalue comprehension in addition to semantic parsing. The experimental results\ndemonstrate the significance of database values in generating accurate text-to-SQLs\nfor big databases. Furthermore, even the most popular and effective text-to-SQL\nmodels, i.e. ChatGPT, only achieve 40.08% in execution accuracy, which is still far\nfrom the human result of 92.96%, proving that challenges still stand. Besides, we\nalso provide an efficiency analysis to offer insights into generating text-to-efficient\u0002SQLs that are beneficial to industries. We believe that BIRD will contribute to\nadvancing real-world applications of text-to-SQL research. The leaderboard and\nsource code are available: https://bird-bench.github.io/.\n1 Introduction\nText-to-SQL parsing [57, 52, 53, 2, 54, 37], which focuses on transforming natural language into SQL\nqueries, has attracted significant research interests from both academia and industry. This attention\nstems from its potential to empower non-expert data analysts in automatically extracting desired\ninformation from ubiquitous relational databases using natural language. Recent advances in neural\n♣ Equal contribution.\n‡Work done during an intern at Alibaba DAMO Academy.\n†Corresponding authors.\narXiv:2305.03111v2 [cs.CL] 30 May 2023\nLarge and Realistic Database Values External Knowledge Reasoning SQL Execution Efficiency\nWhat is the winning rate of Boston Celtics in 2000?\nSELECT COUNT(won) / ((COUNT(won) + COUNT(lose))\nFROM teams WHERE team_name = ‘Boston Celtics’\nAND year = 2000;\nExternal Knowledge:\nwinning rate = # won / (# won + # lose)\nWhat is the average salary of the worst performing managers?\nSELECT AVG(CAST(REPLACE(SUBSTR(T1.salary, 4), ',', '') AS REAL)) FROM\nlast_name\nMilgrom\n… …\nem_id\n0000\n… …\nEmployees\nUS$57,500.00\nfirst_name salary\n2222\n6543\nAdams\nWood\nMilgrom\nSandy\nEmily\n… …\nUS$19,500.00\nUS$69,000.00\n… …\nReasoned Database:\nemployee AS T1 JOIN position AS T2 ON T1.positionID = T2.positionID \nWHERE T1.performance = 'Poor' AND T2.positiontitle = 'Manager'\nAmong the coaches who have served more than 2 NBA teams, during \nwhich coach‘s period of coaching, a team has the least numbers of \ngames lost in the post-season games?\nSELECT coachID FROM coaches WHERE lgID='NBA’ AND post_wins !=0\nSQL1: normal semantic parser\nAND post_losses !=0 AND coachID IN \n(SELECT coachID FROM coaches WHERE lgID='NBA’ GROUP BY coachID \nHAVING COUNT(tmID)\u003e=2) ORDER BY post_losses ASC LIMIT 1 ;\nRun time: 22.4s\nWhat is the average salary of the worst performing managers?\nSELECT AVG(CAST(REPLACE(SUBSTR(T1.salary, 4), ',', '') AS REAL)) FROM\nlast_name\nMilgrom\n… …\nem_id\n0000\n… …\nEmployees\nUS$57,500.00\nfirst_name salary\n2222\n6543\nAdams\nWood\nSanta\nSandy\nEmily\n… …\nUS$19,500.00\nUS$69,000.00\n… …\nReasoned Database:\nemployee AS T1 JOIN position AS T2 ON T1.positionID = T2.positionID \nWHERE T1.performance = 'Poor' AND T2.positiontitle = 'Manager'\nSQL2: efficient semantic parser\nSELECT coachID FROM coaches WHERE lgID=‘NBA’ AND post_wins !=0 \nAND post_losses !=0 AND EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM coaches AS coaches1\nWHERE (coaches1.lgID=‘NBA’) AND (coaches.coachID=coaches1.coachID)\nGROUP BY coaches1.coachID HAVING count(coaches1.tmID) \u003e= 2\nORDER BY NULL ) ORDER BY coaches.post_losses ASC LIMIT 1\nRun time: 4.0s\nHow many accounts are eligible for loans in New York City?\nThe condition of loans is that\nthe type of the account should\nbe “OWNER”.\nSELECT COUNT(*) FROM account WHERE account.type\n= ‘OWNER’ AND city = ‘NY’;\nExternal Knowledge:\n(a). (b).\nHow many accounts are eligible for loans in New York City? (c).\nThe condition of loans is that\nthe type of the account should\nbe “OWNER”.\nSELECT COUNT(*) FROM account WHERE account.type\n= ‘OWNER’ AND disp_id = ‘NY’;\nExternal Knowledge:\nList account id who chooses weekly issue issuance statement?\n‘POPLATEK TYDNE’ stands \nfor weekly issuance.\nSELECT account_id FROM account WHERE account.frequency\n= ‘POPLATEK TYDNE‘;\nExternal Knowledge:\n= ‘OWNER’ AND disp_id = ‘NY’;\nList account id who chooses weekly issue issuance statement?\n‘POPLATEK TYDNE’ stands \nfor weekly issuance.\nSELECT account_id FROM account WHERE account.frequency\n= ‘POPLATEK TYDNE‘;\nExternal Knowledge:\nWhat is the average salary of the worst performing managers?\nFigure 1: Examples of challenges in our BIRD benchmark. 1) databases contain values of noisy data\ntypes [14, 24, 19, 32]. In the left example, the average salary could be fetched by processing the\ndata type from string (TEXT in SQLite) to float (REAL in SQLite) after deleting the special tokens,\n\"US$\" and \",\". 2) external knowledge and reasoning are required. In the middle example, models\nmust handle that only \"OWNER\" accounts are eligible for loans. 3) query execution efficiency needs\nto be considered. In the right example, the adoption of more efficient SQL queries leads to significant\ngains in speed, which is of great value in industries.\nmodels, including those based on large language models (LLMs), have led to impressive performance\non existing benchmarks such as Spider [55] and WikiSQL [60]. For instance, the execution accuracy\nof the top-performing model in Spider leaderboard has increased from 53.5% [61] to 85.3% [35] over\nthe past three years. The latest SOTA parser [35] in Spider benefits from the powerful understanding\nand coding capabilities of the large language model (LLM), and such excellent performance leads us\nto ask a question: Can LLM already serve as a database interface ?\nThe answer is no, as shown in Figure.1, we discovered that current state-of-the-art models still struggle\nto generalize to more realistic situations characterized by large database sizes and noisy content.\nBesides, the mysteries hidden behind the huge database values require external knowledge and\nreasoning to reveal. Furthermore, existing benchmarks do not account for SQL execution efficiency,\nwhich holds significant practical importance in real-life applications, notably in the case of large\ndatabases. Motivated by these observations, we aim to develop a new text-to-SQL benchmark that\nbetter represents real-life scenarios and narrows the gap between experimental and practical settings.\nIn this work, we propose BIRD, a BIg Bench for LaRge-Scale Database Grounded in Text-to-SQLs\nfor real-world applications. BIRD contains complex 12,751 examples of querying information over\n95 big databases with a total size of 33.4 GB spanning 37 professional domains. For training, we\ncollected 80 open-source relational databases from real analysis platforms (Kaggle, Relation.vit);\nfor evaluation, we curated 15 additional relational databases. Given these databases, we rely on\ncrowdsourcing to collect natural language instructions and the corresponding SQLs. First, our\ndatabase experts create a description file explaining all column names, abbreviated values, value\ntypes, and external knowledge for each database to help annotators better understand the database\ncontents. Then we hire and train native speakers to ask questions facing these databases on one\nside; on the other side, a SQL annotation team consisting of data engineers and database students\nis recruited to generate SQLs to answer questions. To accommodate efficiency, we propose a new\nmetric Valid Efficiency Score (VES) to evaluate the efficiency of generated SQLs in addition to the\nstandard execution accuracy. To the best of our knowledge, BIRD is the first text-to-SQL benchmark\nto incorporate efficiency, promoting more efficient query methods within the context of massive and\nnoisy database contents.\nWe evaluate the performance of state-of-the-art text-to-SQL parsers using two popular methodologies:\nfine-tuning with T5 [38], and in-context learning with large language models (LLMs) such as Codex\n[6] (code-davinci-002) and ChatGPT [33] (gpt-3.5-turbo). Our experimental results\nreveal that the current models struggle to generalize well. Specifically, the Spider SOTA model,\nwhich depends solely on the database schema, achieves execution accuracies of only 25.88% and\n28.95% on the development and test sets, respectively. In comparison, the performance still lags far\nbehind human performance, which we also provide in this benchmark. We encourage further research\nto address the more realistic settings presented in this benchmark.\n2\n(c) The Sample Description (a) Annotation Workflow\n(b) Double-blind Annotation\nHow many bioassay signatures … …\nWhat is percentage of male who … …\nWhat is the tag of the album … …\n… …\n… …\nSELECT COUNT(T1.signature_id)FROM … …\nSELECT CAST(SUM(CASE WHEN gender … …\n… …\n… …\nSELECT T2.tag FROM torrents FROM … …\nexperts\nAnnotation Question Crowd\nSQL Crowd\n4\n3\n4\nAnnotation\nAnnotation\n1\nSupervision\nSupervision\nTraining \u0026 Quiz\n2\n3\n8/10\n9/10\nenroll\nenroll\nWhat is percentage of male who … …\nSELECT CAST(SUM(CASE WHEN gender … …\nSELECT CAST(SUM(CASE WHEN gender … …\nMATCH\nSELECT CAST(SUM(CASE WHEN gender … …\nSELECT CAST(SUM(CASE WHEN gender … …\nCheck Until\nQuestion:\nCollect\nSQL1:\nSQL2:\nSQL1:\nSQL2:\nRESULT 1\nRESULT 2\nRESULT 1\nRESULT 2\nDISMATCH\nCheck\nFigure 2: An Overview of the Text-to-SQL Annotation Workflow in (a). This figure depicts a\nfour-step procedure. (1) The workflow begins with specialists assembling and producing databases\nand description files. (2) Experts then teach and evaluate crowdsourcing people, keeping only those\nwho pass the evaluation. (3) Question annotators create a corpus of questions using databases and\ntheir corresponding description files. (4) SQL annotators produce SQL files, equipped with databases,\ndescriptions, and questions. (b) and (c) also depict the Double-blind annotation procedure and an\nexample of database descriptions.\n2 Task Formulation \u0026 Annotations\n2.1 Task Definition.\nText-to-SQL refers to the process of converting a natural language question Q into a SQL query\nY capable of retrieving relevant data from a database. The database D = ⟨C, T ⟩, where C and T\nrepresent columns and tables respectively. External knowledge evidence K can be employed to\nimprove the model’s comprehension of the database contents. Finally, the text-to-SQL could be\nformulated as:\nY = f(Q, D, K | θ), (1)\nwhere f (· | θ) is a model or neural network with the parameter θ. It is possible for external\nknowledge to be structured, as json or csv files in KaggleDBQA [25], and KnowSQL [10], or it\ncould be unstructured, as evidence texts in [21]. In some cases, such as in Spider [55], no external\nknowledge is needed, which is represented as K = {∅}. It’s important for models to determine\nwhether or not to select knowledge by themselves when facing new questions [40], thus we provide\nall three formats of knowledge as bank in BIRD.\n3 Dataset Construction\n3.1 Database Source\nIt is difficult to collect databases with complex schemas and sufficient value. Due to privacy protection,\nthe majority of synthetic databases are not available to individual users as open-source data. Earlier\nworks [46, 55] that choose to self-design database schemas and value production can reduce this\nissue. Nonetheless, the value distribution and schemas may differ from real-world scenarios in this\nway. we obtain databases from three different sources to enrich real-world attributes. All of these\ndatabases contain real and large value distributions and are easily accessible with the appropriate\nlicenses. 32% of our databases are sourced from Kaggle*, a platform renowned for holding data\nscience competitions with difficult, noisy content and schemas. Another 48% come from CTU\nPrague Relational Learning Repository†, an open platform for machine learning research with multi-\n*https://www.kaggle.com/\n†https://relational.fit.cvut.cz/\n3\nrelational data. The remaining 20% are built with a similar procedure to DuSQL [46], which entails\nacquiring open tables, synthesizing and standardizing schemas, and generating database constraints.\nFinally, we present 95 databases consisting of 69, 11, and 15 databases for training, development, and\ntesting respectively. We classified our databases into 37 domains, covering blockchain, sports, health\ncare, politics, etc. Similar to Spider, to safeguard the integrity of the databases, we have manually\nset foreign key and primary key constraints and calibrated integrated names for abbreviated table\nor column names. Our databases encompass schemas and values from a variety of domains. We\nanticipate that it will be a significant resource for researchers to explore domain generalization in\nsemantic parsing tasks with large database contents.\n3.2 Question Annotation\nEntrance. We hire a group of native speakers of English with degrees above the bachelor’s level\nand database-related knowledge to ask a variety of natural language questions regarding the contents\nof databases. To fulfill this objective, we have adopted the following procedure: (1). ER diagrams\nand database description files are documented to assist the annotators in understanding the databases;\n(2). we present the annotators with three databases from different domains and require them to\ngenerate 10 questions for each database; (3). these questions are then assessed by 3 text-to-SQL\nexperts applying predefined rules. Those questions earning at least two votes are marked as valid.\nOnly annotators capable of generating more than 8 valid questions per database are preserved. As a\nresult, 11 native speakers contribute questions to BIRD.\nDatabase Description File. The Database Description File is a crucial resource designed to aid\nannotators in comprehending the structure of databases, thereby allowing them to ask insightful\nquestions about the contents. It offers two primary pieces of information regarding the database.\n(1). Full schema names: Database table and column names are frequently abbreviated, which are\ndifficult to understand. (2). Value description: This is especially beneficial when phrases or tokens\nin a question are not string-matched to values in the database, as Figure 2 shows, it’s very hard for\nthe model to determine the meanings of \"POJISTNE\" stands for insurance payment. The\nDatabase Description File includes information on values via accessing original description files or\nwebsites in order to gather this information.\nExternal Knowledge. In our study of professional data analysis, we find that external knowledge\nis required to map the natural language instructions into counterpart database elements. Therefore,\nwe collect and classify this knowledge into four categories: (1). Numeric Reasoning Knowledge:\nthis category refers to the mathematical computation required for certain SQL operations. In our\nbenchmark, we present 8 basic math operations‡, including 4 operations with additional inference as\n[7]: MINUS, ADDITION, DIVISION, MULTIPLY. BIRD also contains compositional operations\nover basic ones, such as the computation of percentages, ratios, etc. (2). Domain Knowledge: this\ncategory consists of domain-specific knowledge that is utilized to generate SQL operations [10, 59].\nFor instance, a business analyst in the banking business may require knowledge of financial indicators\nsuch as return on investment and net income in order to generate effective SQL queries. (3). Synonym\nKnowledge: this category includes words or expressions that have the same or similar meanings. In\nthe context of our research, synonym knowledge is used to ensure that questions can be correctly\nconverted to SQLs, regardless of how they are phrased differently [11]. (4). Value Illustration:\nwe also provide detailed descriptions of databases values, including value types, value categories;\nand the mapping combinations of columns and values that correspond to entities, for example:\n\"center\" can be represented by \"pos = C\" in the database professional_basketball.\nThis extensive description will assist annotators in comprehending the database’s structures and\ncontents.\n3.3 SQL Annotation\nEntrance. With the purpose of enhancing the quality of our SQL queries, we assemble a team\nof skilled data engineers and database students. The team undergoes rigorous testing through the\ntext-to-SQL evaluation process, which assesses their capability of generating SQL queries for a\n‡AVERAGE, MIN, MAX, SUM belong to basic Aggregation operation in our taxonomy, and COUNT is a specific\ncategory as shown in 3.\n4\nTable 1: An overview comparison between BIRD and other cross-domain text-to-SQL benchmarks.\nIn SQL, Function pertains to the window function. Knowledge refers to whether or not this\ndataset necessitates knowledge reasoning from the model. Efficiency refers to whether or not\nthis dataset takes into consideration execution efficiency.\nDataset # Example # DB # Table/DB # Row/DB Function Knowledge Efficiency\nWikiSQL [60] 80,654 26,521 1 17\nSpider [55] 10,181 200 5.1 2K\nKaggleDBQA [25] 272 8 2.3 280K\nBIRD 12,751 95 7.3 549K\nvariety of questions facing different domains of databases. Each annotator is asked to answer 10\nquestions, and only those who score at least 9 out of 10 will be qualified to annotate SQL queries for\nBIRD.\nDouble-Blind Annotation. As shown in Figure 2 (b), we employ a double-blind technique [43] for\nSQL annotation. This approach involves two independent SQL annotators who generate SQLs for the\nsame question without discussion. The annotated SQLs are executed in databases, and those yielding\nidentical results are gathered§. Otherwise, the SQLs are checked with experts until a consensus is\nreached. Double-blind procedures can dramatically reduce the SQL annotation error rate, as there\nis a small probability for two annotators to generate the same incorrect results when working with\ndatabases with large contents. The most well-formed and efficient SQL¶selected by experts for each\nquestion is picked for the BIRD corpus, and the knowledge evidence sentences are recorded for each\nSQL if utilized.\nExamination. After a meticulous double-blind procedure, experts evaluate each text-to-SQL pair\nto ensure the highest quality of data. The evaluation process includes examining each data in two\ndimensions: SQL validness, and text-knowledge-SQL alignment. Firstly, the SQL validness will be\nconfirmed that each SQL is executable and can return a valid result from the database. The \"valid\nresult\" refers to the set of results that is not \"NULL\". If the executed result set is \"NULL\", experts\nwill make slight changes to the conditions of the questions until the associated SQLs can provide a\nvalid result set. Secondly, text-knowledge-SQL alignment is involved to ensure that each SQL can be\ngenerated with the given texts and knowledge evidence. If the evidence is insufficient to generate the\nSQL or contains errors, experts will be in charge of correcting them.\nDifficulty. In order to help researchers deeply analyze model performance in various text-to-SQL\ncase levels, we class all examples as simple, moderate, and challenging. Spider computed\ndifficulty mainly based on SQL complexity. However, we find that additional factors, such as question\ncomprehension, schema linking, external knowledge acquisition, and reasoning, also influence model\nand human performance when translating texts to SQLs. Therefore, each SQL annotator is required\nto evaluate examples based on these factors, and experts conclude the ratings to divide examples into\nthe three aforementioned difficulty levels, providing a more comprehensive difficulty analysis on\ntext-to-SQL tasks.\n4 Data Statistics\n4.1 Overall Statistics\nTable 1 presents an overview comparison between BIRD and other text-to-SQL benchmarks. As the\nstatistics demonstrate, BIRD is a large-scale cross-domain benchmark, covering a variety of domains.\nBIRD is also a SQL playground, which incorporates the usage of window functions, knowledge\n§To reduce the ambiguity of use of DISTINCT, we compare HashMap outcomes instead of lists.\n¶Definitely, this procedure can also serve to enhance the diversity of SQL queries corresponding to individual\nquestions, thereby reflecting the diverse perspectives on the same questions. Considering the burden of quality\nverification on pairwise SQLs, we just open-source the optimal SQLs as the ground truth at this time. We will\nopen-source full pairwise SQLs if this benchmark receives enough attention.\n5\nreasoning, and efficiency evaluation in SQL generation. Moreover, the databases included in BIRD\nare the most extensive, making it an ideal testing base for bridging the gap between academic use and\nreal-world applications of text-to-SQL.\n4.2 Question Statistics\nFigure 3 displays the extensive question type statistics of BIRD. To facilitate a deeper understanding\nof the distinctions between questions in BIRD and those from other datasets, we classify ques\u0002tions into two macro-categories: Fundamental Type and Reasoning Type, and each contains 4-5\nmicro-categories in detail. The Fundamental Type contains similar questions to those found in\nother text-to-SQL benchmarks. The Reasoning Type contains questions that require external\nknowledge grounding, which is exclusive to BIRD. As shown in Figure 3, the majority of questions\nare marked as match-based, indicating that schema linking, referring to aligning entities in questions\nto tables or columns [44, 1], continues to be the most important topic of text-to-SQL problems, even\nin the harder scenarios. Among the Reasoning Type of questions, there are ample examples that\nrequire domain knowledge and numeric reasoning, making BIRD questions especially challenging. In\naddition, 70% of the questions need value illustrations. This indicates that more real-world questions\nin text-to-SQL applications demand a thorough understanding of database values, which is consistent\nwith our motivation for creating the BIRD benchmark.\n4.3 Database Statistics\nIn the BIRD, we investigate the distribution of database domains, database size, and value types.\nFigure 4 (a) presents a detailed distribution of domains and their counterpart databases in a sunburst\ndiagram for both training and development sets. The width of each semi-circle corresponds to\nthe number of text-to-SQL pairs using the respective database. Figure 4 (a) also shows the size\ndistributions of databases. The deeper color means a larger size of databases, and vice versa. For\nexample, the database Donor is the largest database with 4.5 GB in this dataset. Furthermore, we\nobserve from Figure 4 (b) that a considerable proportion of BIRD’s data comprises date-related\nvalues. Considering that real-world applications often rely on time-sensitive data [26], the prevalence\nof datetime expressions and their related questions in the dataset we collected highlights the practical\npurposes.\n4.4 SQL Statistics\nOur study provides insights into the complexity and diversity of SQLs used in the BIRD. As illustrated\nin Figure 5, we present a comprehensive distribution analysis of SQLs across four dimensions. No.\nToks / SQL and No. JOINs / SQL demonstrate the intricacy and requirements of multi-table\nreasoning of the SQLs in BIRD. No. of Keywords and No. n-grams / SQL (n=3)\nserve as the support for the diverse patterns of SQLs contained within the dataset since we decouple\nthe question and SQL annotation procedures to make the situation more realistic [5].\n5 Evaluation Metrics\nIn contexts of practical data analysis, text-to-SQL models are prioritized for delivering expected\nresults accurately and efficiently. Thus we provide two metrics in BIRD, execution accuracy (EX)\nand valid efficiency score (VES) to evaluate text-to-SQL parsers confronted with real-world scenarios\nwith large database contents.\n(1) Execution Accuracy (EX): EX is defined as the proportion of questions in the evaluation set for\nwhich the execution results of both the predicted and ground-truth inquiries are identical, relative to\nthe overall number of queries [37]. Considering the result set as Vn executed by the n\nth ground-truth\nSQL Yn, and the result set Vˆ\nn executed by the predicted SQL Yˆn, EX can be computed by:\nEX = Σ\nN\nn=11(Vn, Vˆn)\nN\n, (2)\nwhere 1(·) is an indicator function, which can be represented as:\n1(V, Vˆ ) = (\n1, V = Vˆ\n0, V ̸= Vˆ\n(3)\n6\nQuestion Type Question / SQL Percentage\nFundamental\nRanking\nHow many gas stations in CZE has Premium gas?\nSELECT COUNT(GasStationID) FROM gasstations\nWHERE Country = 'CZE' AND Segment = 'Premium'\nSub Type\nType\nMatch-based\nWhat are the titles of the top 5 posts with the highest \npopularity?\nSELECT Title FROM posts ORDER BY ViewCount DESC \nLIMIT 5\nComparison How many color cards with no borders have been ranked \nhigher than 12000 on EDHRec?\nSELECT COUNT(id) FROM cards WHERE edhrecRank \n\u003e 12000 AND borderColor = 'borderless'\nCounting How many of the members' hometowns are from \nMaryland state?\nSELECT COUNT(T2.member_id) FROM zip_code AS T1\nINNER JOIN member AS T2 ON T1.zip_code = T2.zip\nWHERE T1.state = 'Maryland'\nAggregation\nName the ID and age of patient with two or more\nlaboratory examinations which show their hematoclit level\nexceeded the normal range.\nWhat is the average height of the superheroes from \nMarvel Comics?\nSELECT AVG(T1.height_cm) FROM superhero AS T1 \nINNER JOIN publisher AS T2 ON T1.publisher_id = \nT2.id WHERE T2.publisher_name = 'Marvel Comics'\nReasoning Domain\nKnowledge\nSELECT T1.ID, STRFTIME('%Y', CURRENT_TIMESTAMP)\n- STRFTIME('%Y', T1.Birthday) FROM Patient AS\nT1 INNER JOIN Laboratory AS T2 ON T1.ID =\nT 2 . The Fundamental Type of\nquestions are comparable to other text-to-SQL benchmarks. The Reasoning Type of questions\nrequires external knowledge grounding to answer.\n(2) Valid Efficiency Score (VES): VES is designed to measure the efficiency of valid SQLs generated\nby models. It is worth noting that the term \"valid SQLs\" refers to predicted SQL queries whose\nresult sets align with those of the ground-truth SQLs. Any SQL queries that fail to execute with the\ncorrect values will be declared invalid since they are totally useless if they cannot fulfill the user\nrequests, regardless of their efficiency. In this case, the VES metric could consider both the efficiency\nand accuracy of execution results, providing a comprehensive evaluation of a model’s performance.\n7\nretail\nmovie
