And b) shows the data type distribution of databases.\nNo. Toks / SQL No. of Keywords No. n-grams / SQL (n=3) No. JOINs / SQL\nWikiSQL Spider KaggleDBQA Bird\nFigure 5: A comparative statistical analysis of SQL queries in the BIRD dataset and other cross\u0002domain text-to-SQL benchmarks.\nFormally, the VES can be expressed as:\nVES = Σ\nN\nn=11(Vn, Vˆn) · R(Yn, Yˆn)\nN\n, R(Yn, Yˆn) = s\nE(Yn)\nE(Yˆn)\n(4)\nwhere R(·) denotes the relative execution efficiency of predicted SQL in comparison to ground-truth\nSQL, allowing for machine status-related uncertainty. E(·) is a function to measure the absolute\nexecution efficiency for each SQL in a given environment||. Furthermore, we incorporate the square\nroot function to minimize random instances which are super more rapid or slower than the ground\u0002truth SQLs, i.e. more than 500 times faster or slower. This operation aims to promote the model\nthat can consistently generate efficient SQL predictions by learned expertise of database knowledge,\nrather than relying on arbitrary attempts and guesses for specific scenarios. Here, the efficiency can\nrefer to running time, throughput, memory cost, or merged metrics. In BIRD, we consider the running\ntime as the main metric of efficiency at this time.\n||In BIRD evaluation, we run 100 times for each SQL in the same CPU and evaluate average results after\ndropping the outliers.\n8\nICL Prompt Input\nCREATE TABLE singer\n(\n singer_id TEXT not null\n primary key,\n nation TEXT not null,\n sname TEXT null,\n dname TEXT null,\n cname TEXT null,\n age INTEGER not null,\n year INTEGER not null,\n birth_year INTEGER null,\n salary REAL null,\n city TEXT null,\n phone_number INTEGER null,\n tax REAL null,\n)\n-- External Knowledge: age = year - birth_year;\n-- Using valid SQLite and understading External \nKnowledge, answer the following questions for \nthe tables provided above.\nThe final SQL is: Let's think step by step.\n1. referring to external knowledge, we need\nto filter singers 'by year' - 'birth_year' \u003e 27; 2.\nwe should find out the singers of step 1 in\nwhich nation = 'US', 3. use COUNT() to count\nhow many singers. Finally the SQL is: SELECT\nCOUNT(*) FROM singer WHERE year -\nbirth_year \u003e 27; \n1-shot pseudo example\nCREATE TABLE customers\n(\n CustomerID INTEGER UNIQUE not null\n primary key,\n Segment TEXT null,\n Currency TEXT null\n)\nCREATE TABLE gasstations\n(\n GasStationID INTEGER UNIQUE not null\n primary key,\n ChainID INTEGER null,\n Country TEXT null,\n Segment TEXT null\n)\nCREATE TABLE products\n(\nCREATE TABLE customers\n(\n CustomerID INTEGER UNIQUE not null\n primary key,\n Segment TEXT null,\n Currency TEXT null\n)\nCREATE TABLE gasstations\n(\n GasStationID INTEGER UNIQUE not null\n primary key,\n ChainID INTEGER null,\n Country TEXT null,\n Segment TEXT null\n)\nCREATE TABLE yearmonth\n(\n CustomerID INTEGER not null,\n Date INTEGER not null,\n Consumption REAL null,\n primary key (CustomerID, Date),\n foreign key (CustomerID) references \n customers (CustomerID)\n)\n…\n…\nCreate DDL Prompt\n-- External Knowledge: August of 2012 means Date contains \n'201208' in the yearmonth.date of the database; Price per unit \nof product = T1.Price / T1.Amount\n-- Using valid SQLite and understading External Knowledge, \nanswer the following questions for the tables provided above.\n-- What is the highest eligible free rate for K-12 students in the \nschools in Alameda County?\n-- External Knowledge: August of 2012 means\nDate contains '201208' in the yearmonth.date\nof the database; Price per unit of product =\nPrice / Amount\n-- Using valid SQLite and understading External\nKnowledge, answer the following questions for\nthe tables provided above.\n-- For all the people who paid more than 29.00\nper unit of product id No.5. Give their\nconsumption status in the August of 2012.\nThe final SQL is: Let's think step by step.\\n1. referring to external knowledge, we need to \ncalculate the price per unit of product id No.5, which is T1.Price / T1.Amount; 2. we should \nfilter the people who paid more than 29.00 per unit of product id No.5; 3. we should find out \nthe consumption status of the people in August of 2012. Finally the SQL is: SELECT \nyearmonth.Consumption FROM transactions_1k AS T1 JOIN yearmonth ON T1.CustomerID = \nyearmonth.CustomerID AND T1.Date = yearmonth.Date WHERE T1.ProductID = 5 AND T1.Price \nThe final SQL is: Let's think step by step.\n1. referring to external knowledge, we need\nto calculate the price per unit of product id\nNo.5, which is Price / Amount; 2. we should\nfilter the people who paid more than 29.00\nper unit of product id No.5; 3. we should find\nout the consumption status of the people in\nAugust of 2012.\nSELECT yearmonth.Consumption FROM transactions_1k JOIN yearmonth ON transactions_1k.CustomerID = yearmonth.CustomerID\nAND transactions_1k.Date = yearmonth.Date WHERE transactions_1k.ProductID = 5 AND transactions_1k.Price /\ntransactions_1k.Amount \u003e 29.00 AND yearmonth.Date = ‘201208’;\nKnowledge Prompt\nTask Instruction\n1-shot COT Prompt\nChatGPT COT Steps\nQuestion Prompt\nSQL Output\nFinally the SQL is:\nFigure 6: The detailed prompt design for implementation of ChatGPT + KG + COT.\n6 Experiments\n6.1 Baseline Models\nWe present the performance of two types of baseline models in BIRD. The first type of model is\nbased on fine-tuning (FT) techniques, which outputs SQL tuning all parameters of language models\nvia annotated data. On the other hand, in-context learning (ICL) is employed in the second type of\nmodel. In FT models, we select T5 [38] as the main baseline models. While seq2AST-based methods\n[44, 4] are also effective in text-to-SQL, actually their grammar rules utilized during decoding\nare constrained on specific datasets [26]. For ICL-based models, we provide zero-shot results of\nCodex, ChatGPT and its Chain-Of-Thought (COT) demonstration, which are very good at zero-shot\ntext-to-SQL generation.\nFT-based Models. T5 is a strong and versatile pre-trained language model (PLM) for text\u0002to-text generation that has achieved state-of-the-art performance in a variety of semantic parsing\ntasks, including text-to-SQL. We concatenate the question with serialized database schema as input\n[41, 51, 42]. And SQL can be fetched in an end-to-end fashion by easily fine-tuning.\nICL-based Models. Codex (code-davinci-002**††) and ChatGPT (gpt-3.5-turbo) are\npopular and powerful large-scale pre-trained language models (LLMs) for code generation driven\nby ICL. It can produce multiple types of codes, including SQL, from human instructions without\nadditional training. We employ programming-based prompts, as described in [39], to collect results\nby calling the API. Given that models are not allowed to unseen databases and ground-truth SQLs in\nthe evaluation set, a zero-shot generation strategy is the most appropriate. Moreover, to investigate\nthe impact of multi-step reasoning of LLMs on BIRD, we implement the Chain-Of-Thought (COT)\n**https://openai.com/blog/openai-api\n††It’s still valid at the time of writing.\n9\ntechnique [50] by easily adding the prompt sentence \"Let’s think step by step.\" before\nthe generation of SQLs [22]. However, we find out the output of ChatGPT is too uncertain with\nmany unexpected explanations, thus we provide a 1-shot pseudo example for ChatGPT to learn the\nprocedure of thinking and output format. The detailed prompt design is shown in Figure 6\nKnowledge Fusion. In the baseline implementation, we naively concatenate the knowledge evi\u0002dence sentences with questions and database schemas, but we can observe a significant improvement\nby this easy method. More complicated and effective strategy of knowledge grounding for ChatGPT\nand T5 would be an important future topic. The knowledge evidence sentences are concluded to the\nexternal knowledge provided by annotators as described in Section 3.2.\n6.2 Execution Accuracy Analysis\nAs shown in the Table 2, we implement each baseline model for both two scenarios. The first is NOT\nto provide the ground truth knowledge evidence sentence for each text-to-SQL data. The other testing\nbed is to provide knowledge evidence and make tex-to-SQL models do knowledge grounding by\nthemselves, which is more challenging.\nResults w/o Knowledge Evidence. As shown in the Table 3, the performance of FT models T5\nperform worse obviously than ICL-based LLMs in both EX and VES. This indicates that LLM with a\nhuge number of parameters and emergent capability [49] can have a better self-knowledge grounding\ncapability and pre-trained SQL knowledge than FT smaller models with less than 5B parameters.\nAfter being equipped with COT, ChatGPT can perform better, since multi-step reasoning is beneficial\nto it when the knowledge and data is low-resource.\nResults w/ Knowledge Evidence. After being easily fed with the external knowledge about the\ndatabase contents, all models have a clear improvement across the different difficulty levels as shown\nin Table 2 and Table 4. This indicates that knowledge evidence in BIRD is effective and instructive\nfor model to better understand the database contents. All these performance gains can illustrate\nthat the database contents are very important to text-to-SQL models when facing more real cases.\nDespite this, we observe a decline or limited improvements in performance for ChatGPT + knowledge\ngrounding of database contents annotated by human experts for COT version. We hypothesis that\nthe internal multi-step knowledge reasoning of LLMs is not compatible with the way of external\nknowledge in this situation. Therefore, the development of a method that effectively combines the\nstrong multi-step self-reasoning capabilities of LLMs with external knowledge reasoning coherently\npresents a promising future direction [30]. This innovation may pave the way for the LLM plugin\nthat facilitates integration of different sources of knowledge for more reliable results.\nTable 2: The Execution Accuracy (EX) of SOTA text-to-SQL models in BIRD. The human perfor\u0002mance is also provided.\nModels Development Data Testing Data\nw/o knowledge w/ knowledge w/o knowledge w/ knowledge\nFT-based\nT5-Base 6.32 11.54 (+5.22) 7.06 12.89 (+5.83)\nT5-Large 9.71 19.75 (+10.04) 10.38 20.94 (+10.56)\nT5-3B 10.37 23.34 (+12.97) 11.17 24.05 (+12.88)\nICL-based\nCodex 25.42 34.35 (+8.93) 24.86 36.47 (+11.61)\nChatGPT 24.05 37.22 (+13.17) 26.77 39.30 (+12.53)\nChatGPT + COT 25.88 36.64 (+10.76) 28.95 40.08 (+11.24)\nHuman Performance - - 72.37 92.96 (+20.59)\n6.3 Efficiency Analysis\nAs discussed in Section 5, we introduced a novel sub-task, text-to-efficient-SQL, and proposed the\nVES metric for its evaluation. In this section, we at first present overall efficiency performance of\n10\nTable 3: The Valid Efficiency Score (VES) of SOTA text-to-SQL models in BIRD. The human\nperformance is also presented.\nModels Development Data Testing Data\nw/o knowledge w/ knowledge w/o knowledge w/ knowledge\nFT-based\nT5-Base 7.78 12.90 (+5.12) 8.97 14.71 (+5.74)\nT5-Large 9.90 22.74 (+12.84) 12.25 25.00 (+12.75)\nT5-3B 13.62 25.57 (+11.95) 15.17 27.80 (+12.63)\nICL-based\nCodex 33.37 43.41 (+10.04) 35.40 41.60 (+6.20)\nChatGPT 27.97 43.81 (+15.84) 36.68 51.40 (+14.72)\nChatGPT + COT 32.33 42.30 (+9.97) 49.69 56.56 (+6.87)\nHuman Performance - - 70.36 90.27 (+19.91)\nMODEL\nDEV SET TEST SET\nsimple moderate challenging total simple moderate challenging total\n(EX) ChatGPT 31.08 13.29 12.08 24.05 35.41 19.46 12.28 26.77\n(EX) ChatGPT + KG 45.44 26.14 19.01 37.22 49.21 31.89 20.70 39.30\n(VES) ChatGPT 36.20 15.43 14.42 27.97 50.09 24.71 15.39 36.68\n(VES) ChatGPT + KG 54.71 28.16 22.80 43.81 65.06 41.21 25.81 51.40\nTable 4: The Execution Accuracy (EX) and Valid Efficiency Score (VES) are presented for both\nthe ChatGPT model and its version with knowledge grounding (KG), taking into consideration\ndevelopment and testing datasets.\nbaseline models. Afterwards, we investigate two prospective approaches for enhancing text-to-SQL\nsystems to generate more efficient SQL queries.\nGeneral Results. According to the Table 3, we can observe that the text-to-SQL models with higher\nEX can more possibly achieve higher VES. This relationship can be explained by the prerequisite\nthat text-to-SQL models must accurately predict results in order to attain a higher VES, which fulfills\nthe pratical purpose.\nTwo-Stage Optimization. Intuitively, we can decompose the goal of text-to-efficient-SQL into\ntwo sub-procedures. The first is semantic parsing procedure as previous text-to-SQL task, which only\nconsiders how to convert questions into SQLs accurately. The second procedure is SQL optimization\nto rewrite the SQLs to be more efficient while keeping the same results [63]. In order to demonstrate\nthe effectiveness of this workflow, we randomly pick 10 examples from the development set in which\nChatGPT accurately predicts the results, then our experts rewrite them into more efficient SQLs by\nquery optimization rules [29, 34, 64].\nGiven that all these SQLs can fetch the correct results generated by ChatGPT, then we only present\nthe time saving percentage to indicate the effectiveness of query optimization. The detailed analysis\nwith examples is shown in the Figure 7. It includes the optimized SQLs, running time comparison,\nand reasons to describe the counterpart rule. The future work also could be a one-stage learning that\ncan take into consideration the value descriptions of databases, while generating SQLs.\nEmbodied Database. In BIRD, We pioneer the concept of an embodied database allowing models\nto be aware of data types and distributions by producing SQL queries that interact with databases.\nThis method establishes the groundwork for creating more effective and efficient SQL queries. For\nexample, based on the cases from the Figure 7, the SQL effciency could also be improved obviously\nafter setting indexes in the database.\n11\nQuery Rewriting\nEx1.1 Question:\nList out the age of users who located in Vienna, Austria obtained the badge?\nChatGPT SQL:\nSELECT Age FROM users WHERE Location = 'Vienna, Austria‘ AND Id IN (SELECT UserId FROM \nbadges)\nOptimized SQL: (time-saving percentage: 99.92%)\nSELECT u.Age FROM users AS u INNER JOIN badges AS b ON u.Id = b.UserId WHERE u.Location\n= 'Vienna, Austria’\nTake Away:\nBy applying a JOIN operation instead of a subquery with IN can improve efficiency, as the database may execute the JOIN and \nfiltering processes concurrently in just one operation without the need to store the intermediate results to filter primary query.\nEx1.2 Question:\nHow many of the members' hometowns are from Maryland state?\nChatGPT SQL:\nSELECT COUNT(*) FROM member INNER JOIN zip_code ON member.zip = zip_code.zip_code WHERE \nzip_code.state = 'Maryland’\nOptimized SQL: (time-saving percentage: 67.93%)\nSELECT COUNT(member.member_id) FROM member INNER JOIN zip_code ON member.zip = \nzip_code.zip_code WHERE zip_code.state = 'Maryland’\nTake Away:\nUtilizing the COUNT function on a NOT-NULL column, as opposed to COUNT(*), can increase time efficiency. This rewritten\nSQL enables the database to count NOT-NULL values within a single column, rather than compute all rows including those with \nNULL values. Usually, the primary key column is selected as this NOT-NULL column.\nEx1.3 Question:\nWho is the owner of the account with the largest loan amount?\nChatGPT SQL:\nSELECT c.client_id FROM client c INNER JOIN disp d ON c.client_id = d.client_id INNER \nJOIN loan l ON d.account_id = l.account_id ORDER BY l.amount DESC LIMIT 1\nOptimized SQL: (time-saving percentage: 62.39%)\nSELECT c.client_id FROM client c INNER JOIN disp d ON c.client_id = d.client_id\nINNER JOIN loan l ON d.account_id = l.account_id WHERE l.amount = ( SELECT MAX(amount) \nFROM loan)\nTake Away:\nIn an unindexed environment, employing the MAX function can potentially yield faster results since it avoids the need for sorting,\nwhich could run against a large table.\nAdding Indexes to Database\nEx2.1 Question:\nHow many accounts are there in the district of \\\"Pisek\\\"?\nChatGPT SQL:\nSELECT COUNT(*) FROM account a INNER JOIN district d ON a.district_id = d.district_id\nWHERE d.A2 = ‘Pisek’\nAdded Indexes: (time-saving percentage: 87.27%)\nCREATE INDEX account_district_id_index ON account(district_id); \nCREATE UNIQUE INDEX district_district_id_uindex ON district(district_id); \nTake Away:\nAdding indexes into a database can significantly increase the speed of SQL queries because it creates a data structure that enables \nthe database engine to quickly locate rows that match specific criteria instead of scanning the entire table.\nFigure 7: Two possible solutions and explanations to improve efficiency are presented. The first\nbatch of examples shows how to optimize SQL efficiency by rewriting SQL based on rules. The\nlast example is to show that adding indexes to databases can also improve SQL efficiency without\nrewriting them.\n6.4 Human Performance\nIn order to active the efforts of text-to-SQL studies to achieve an application-level performance in\nthe challenging but closer to the real-world scenarios, we also provide human performance in BIRD.\nThe Table 2, Table 3 shows that there’s still a huge gap between even SOTA text-to-SQL models and\nhuman performance.\nThe procedure of collecting human performance is still rigorous. During the annotation, all data\nis divided into 10 batches for better management and error tracks by experts. The first 8 batchs of\n12\nWrong Schema Linking (41.6%)\nQuestion:\nList the names of schools with more than 30 differences\nin enrollments between K-12 and ages 5-17. Please also \ngive the full street address of the schools.\nEvidence:\nDifference in enrollment = &#96;Enrollment (K-12)&#96; -\n&#96;Enrollment (Ages 5-17)&#96;\nGround Truth:\nSELECT T1.School, T1.StreetAbr FROM \nschools AS T1 INNER JOIN frpm AS T2 ON \nT1.CDSCode = T2.CDSCode WHERE \nT2.&#96;Enrollment (K-12)&#96; -\nT2.&#96;Enrollment (Ages 5-17)&#96; \u003e 30\nChatGPT SQL:\nSELECT s.School, s.Street, s.City,\ns.Zip FROM schools s JOIN frpm f ON \ns.CDSCode = f.CDSCode WHERE\nf.&#96;Enrollment (K-12)&#96; - f.&#96;Enrollment\n(Ages 5-17)&#96; \u003e 30\nQuestion:\nList out the accounts who have the earliest trading \ndate in 1995.\nEvidence:\nFalse\nGround Truth:\nSELECT account_id FROM trans WHERE \nSTRFTIME('%Y', date) = '1995' ORDER \nBY date ASC LIMIT 1\nChatGPT SQL:\nSELECT account_id FROM account WHERE\ndate = (SELECT MIN(date) FROM \naccount WHERE date LIKE '1995%')\nQuestion:\nWhat is the average math score of the school with the \nlowest average score for all subjects, and in which county \nis it located?\nEvidence:\nAverage score for all subjects can be computed by \nAvgScrMath + AvgScrRead + AvgScrWrite\nGround Truth:\nSELECT T1.AvgScrMath, T2.County FROM \nsatscores AS T1 INNER JOIN schools AS \nT2 ON T1.cds = T2.CDSCode WHERE \nT1.AvgScrMath IS NOT NULL ORDER BY \nT1.AvgScrMath + T1.AvgScrRead + \nT1.AvgScrWrite ASC LIMIT 1\nChatGPT SQL:\nSELECT cname, AvgScrMath FROM satscores\nJOIN schools ON satscores.cds = \nschools.CDSCode WHERE satscores.rtype = \n‘S’ ORDER BY (AvgScrMath + AvgScrRead + \nAvgScrWrite) ASC LIMIT 1\nMisunderstanding Database Content (40.8%)\nQuestion:\nWhat is the average lap time in milliseconds of all the lap \nrecords set on the various circuits in Italy?\nEvidence:\naverage = AVG(milliseconds)\nGround Truth:\nSELECT CAST(SUM(T1.milliseconds) AS \nREAL) / COUNT(T1.lap) FROM lapTimes AS \nT1 INNER JOIN races AS T2 on T1.raceId \n= T2.raceId INNER JOIN circuits AS T3 \non T2.circuitId = T3.circuitId WHERE \nT3.country = 'Italy’\nChatGPT SQL:\nSELECT AVG(milliseconds) FROM \nlap_records WHERE circuit IN ('Monza', \n'Imola', 'Mugello', 'Misano')\nQuestion:\nOn average, how much did the Student_Club spend on \nfood for the typical event in the past?\nEvidence:\nDIVIDE(SUM(spent), COUNT(spent)) where category = \n'Food'; 'event in the past' means event_status = 'Closed’\nGround Truth:\nSELECT SUM(spent) / COUNT(spent) FROM \nbudget WHERE category = 'Food' AND \nevent_status = 'Closed’\nChatGPT SQL:\nSELECT DIVIDE(SUM(spent), COUNT(spent)) \nFROM budget WHERE category = 'Food' AND \nevent_status = 'Closed'\nMisunderstanding Knowledge Evidence (17.6%)\nQuestion :\nName elementary schools in Riverside which the average \nof average math score for SAT is grater than 400, what is \nthe funding type of these schools?\nEvidence:\nAverage of average math = sum(average math scores) / \ncount(schools)\nGround Truth:\nSELECT T1.sname, T2.&#96;Charter Funding \nType&#96; FROM satscores AS T1 INNER JOIN \nfrpm HAVING CAST(SUM(T1.AvgScrMath) \nAS REAL) / COUNT(T1.cds) \u003e 400\nChatGPT SQL:\nSELECT s.School, s.FundingType FROM\nschools s AND sc.rtype = ‘E’ AND \nsc.AvgScrMath \u003e 400 GROUP BY s.School, \ns.FundingType\nQuestion:\nHow many meeting events were held in 2020?\nSyntax Error (3.0%)\nQuestion:\nHow much more was customer 7 consuming in April 2013 \nthan customer 5?\nEvidence:\nApril 2013 refers to ‘201304’ in the yearmonth.date\nGround Truth:\nSELECT SUM(IIF(CustomerID = 7, \nConsumption, 0)) - SUM(IIF(CustomerID = \n5, Consumption, 0)) FROM yearmonth\nWHERE Date = '201304’\nChatGPT SQL:\nSELECT7, (SELECT Consumption FROM \nyearmonth WHERE CustomerID = 7 AND Date \n= 201304) - (SELECT Consumption FROM \nyearmonth WHERE CustomerID = 5 AND Date \n= 201304)\nEvidence:\nmeeting events refers to type = 'Meeting'; held in 2020 \nrefers to YEAR(event_date) = 2020\nGround Truth:\nSELECT COUNT(event_id) FROM event \nWHERE type = 'Meeting' AND \nSTRFTIME('%Y', COLUMN) = ‘2020’\nChatGPT SQL:\nSELECT COUNT(*) FROM event WHERE type \n= 'Meeting' AND YEAR(event_date) = \n2020\n…\n…\nFigure 8: 4 major types of error cases are presented. Some cases are shortcut for better presentation.\ndata is the final training data and dev data for public use, and the remaining 2 batches of data is\nused for testing. We consider the annotation of first 8 batches of data as a learning processes for\nSQL annotators since their erroneous SQLs could be fixed by experts and learn how to generate\ngood-quality SQLs for this task. Then their first scores on an examination, conducted by testing\nset from the final two batches can be viewed as the human performance since we don’t interrupt\nand assist them during examination and all errors are preserved. After testing, we proceed with the\nfollowing double-blind SQL annotation procedures as Section 3.3 to correct SQLs for these data\nby discussion with experts. And SQLs after second round double-blind annotation are collected as\nground truth.\n6.5 Error Analysis\nChatGPT (GPT-3.5-turbo) is widely acknowledged as one of the most potent code-based large\nlanguage models in existence and can achieve the SOTA result in BIRD. Therefore, in this error\nanalysis as shown in the Figure 8, the performance of ChatGPT is concentrated. We observe 500\nrandomly sampled error cases, providing a detailed assessment in the following categories. Wrong\nSchema Linking (41.6%) pertains to the scenario where ChatGPT can accurately comprehend\nthe structure of the database, but erroneously associates it with inappropriate columns and tables.\nThis demonstrates that the task of schema linking [44, 59], even in intricate and practical situations,\ncontinues to be a significant obstacle for text-to-SQL models. Misunderstanding Database Content\n(40.8%) occurs when ChatGPT either fails to recall the correct database structure (e.g., rtype\ndoesn’t belong to the satscores table) or generates fake schema items (e.g., lap_records is not\nappearing in the formula_1 database and many values are predicted incorrectly) especially when\nthe database is very large. In this case, how to make ChatGPT really understand database structure and\ncontents [28] is still a pain point topic in LLMs. Misunderstanding Knowledge Evidence (17.6%)\nrefers to cases in which ChatGPT does not accurately interpret human-annotated evidence. An\ninstance is that ChatGPT directly copies the formula DIVIDE(SUM(spent), COUNT(spent)).\nThis finding demonstrates that ChatGPT exhibits a lack of robustness in response to unfamiliar\nprompts or knowledge, causing it to directly replicate formulas without considering SQL syntax.\nAdditionally, this vulnerability may lead to security concerns. For example, if a poison knowledge\nevidence is introduced through an unfamiliar formula on purpose, ChatGPT may inadvertently copy\nit into the output without verification, thus exposing the system to potential data security risks [15].\nInstead, humans can understand it and generalize it to SQL via their knowledge and experiences.\nThe final groups of error cases are Syntax Error (3.0%) with a small portion, suggesting that\nthe ChatGPT is well-performed zero-shot semantic parser. However, we observe that ChatGPT\n13\noccasionally employs incorrect keywords (e.g., misusing the MySQL Year() function instead of an\nSQLite function STRFTIME() or exhibits decoding errors (e.g., SELECT7)).\n7 Related Work\nText-to-SQL Dataset A high-quality dataset is essential for driving the development of various\nnatural language processing tasks, including text-to-SQL. The earlier single-domain text-to-SQL\ndatasets such as GeoQuery [57], ATIS [9], and Restaurant [20] were designed for specific information\nretrieval tasks. The WikiSQL [60] and Spider [55], comprising of more than 10,000 data, are more\nappropriate for the development of models that prioritize SQL and database knowledge as opposed to\nthe only memorization of domain knowledge or values.\nDespite these improvements, the majority of cross-domain text-to-SQL datasets continue to focus\non the database schema as opposed to the database values, thereby resulting in a large gap from the\nreal-world scenarios. KaggleDBQA [25] attempted to mitigate this issue by constructing 272 text\u0002to-SQL pairs from eight databases on the open-source data analysis platform Kaggle. Other recent\ndatasets, such as EHRSQL [26], SEDE [13], and MIMICSQL [47], collected databases with diverse\nand large values. Additionally, the SQL queries in these datasets have become more professional,\nwith features like window functions and time-aware grounding. However, these datasets still focus on\na single domain, such as healthcare or software platforms.\nAnother group of recent work starts to concentrate on creating knowledge-intensive text-to-SQL\nbenchmarks [10, 59]. These datasets assist experts in making informed decisions via knowledge\ngrounding or commonsense reasoning in real-world analysis scenarios. To our best knowledge, the\nBIRD represents the first large-scale benchmark to incorporate all of the aforementioned real-world\nfeatures, with a particular emphasis on prioritizing the database values.\nText-to-SQL Models The fundamental principle of a cross-domain text-to-SQL parser involves the\nconstruction of an encoder to learn representations of questions and schemas, followed by a decoder\nto generate SQLs [37]. For example, IRNET [12] designs an encoder consisting of attention-based\nBi-LSTM for learning question and schema representations, and a decoder to predict SQLs based on\nthe encoded intermediate representations. RATSQL [44], SDSQL [17], LGESQL [4], and S2SQL\n[18], Proton [45] enhance the representation learning of natural language questions and database\nschema via relational graph neural network. R2SQL [16], SCORE [56], and STAR [3] enhance\ncontextual learning for conversational text-to-SQL tasks.\nLater, sequence-to-sequence pre-trained language models (PLMs) such as T5 [38] become popular\nin text-to-SQL tasks due to their portability and capability of generation across different datasets.\nThese models achieve impressive results by fine-tuning with minimal effort. Furthermore, RASAT\n[36] enhances T5’s structural information encoding via schema alignment into the encoder, while\nGraphix [28] equip T5 with multi-hop reasoning to achieve state-of-the-art results on complicated\ncross-domain text-to-SQL tasks.\nIn recent years, LLMs such as ChatGPT [33], Palm [8], OPT [58], have attracted considerable\nattention due to their powerful zero-shot reasoning and domain generalization capabilities. ChatGPT\ncan perform exceptionally well on semantic parsing tasks, including text-to-SQL tasks, with minimal\nor no input data. In fact, in the BIRD project, ChatGPT even performs more impressively than\ninitially expected.\nSQL Efficiency Efficient execution of SQL queries on big databases has been a significant topic in\nboth academia and industries. Many techniques are proposed to improve SQL query efficiency, by\nindex selection [23], SQL optimization [27, 63], etc.\nSQL optimiztion is a common method for enhancing the efficacy of SQL queries. Several SQL\noptimization algorithms [29, 31, 48], such as rule-based optimization and cost-based optimization,\nare proved effective. Rule-based optimization employs a set of principles to transform the SQL\nquery into a form that can be executed more efficiently. On the other hand, cost-based optimization\nestimates the execution cost of various query plans and selects the one with the lowest cost through\nanalyzing statistic distribution of database contents. Similar to NLP community, there are also recent\nworks utilizing artificial intelligence for query optimization such as [63].\n14\nIndex prediction is another important technique for improving SQL execution efficiency. Researchers\npropose many algorithms of index prediction [62] based on various optimization criteria, such as\nminimizing SQL execution time, maximizing index utilization.\nHowever, the evaluation and improvement of SQL queries embedded in the text-to-SQL tasks is more\nchallenging since the accuracy of question \u0026 answering (QA) should also be taken into consideration.\nIn this work, we provide VES to measure efficiency of text-to-SQL generators to mitigate this problem.\nIn addition, we suggest that exploring the trade-off between efficiency and execution accuracy could\nbe a promising avenue for future research, as it is beneficial to real-world analysis scenarios.\n8 Conclusion\nIn this paper, we introduce BIRD, an English large-scale cross-domain, text-to-SQL benchmark with\na particular focus on large database contents. BIRD mitigates the gap between text-to-SQL research\nand real-world applications by exploring three additional challenges: 1) handling large and dirty\ndatabase values, 2) external knowledge reasoning, and 3) optimizing SQL execution efficiency. Our\nexperimental results demonstrate that BIRD presents a more daunting challenge compared to existing\nbenchmarks since even the most powerful codeLM, ChatGPT, falls significantly short of human\nperformance. This leaves plenty of room for improvement and innovation in the text-to-SQL tasks.\nMoreover, our thorough efficiency and error analyses provide valuable insights and directions for\nfuture research, paving the way for the development of more advanced and practical text-to-SQL\nsolutions in real-world scenarios.\nReferences\n[1] Ben Bogin, Jonathan Berant, and Matt Gardner. {
  "markdown": "Spider: A Large-Scale Human-Labeled Dataset for Complex and\nCross-Domain Semantic Parsing and Text-to-SQL Task\nTao Yu Rui Zhang Kai Yang Michihiro Yasunaga\nDongxu Wang Zifan Li James Ma Irene Li\nQingning Yao Shanelle Roman Zilin Zhang Dragomir R. Radev\nDepartment of Computer Science, Yale University\n{tao.yu, r.zhang, k.yang, michihiro.yasunaga, dragomir.radev}@yale.edu\nAbstract\nWe present Spider, a large-scale, complex\nand cross-domain semantic parsing and text\u0002to-SQL dataset annotated by 11 college stu\u0002dents. It consists of 10,181 questions and\n5,693 unique complex SQL queries on 200\ndatabases with multiple tables, covering 138\ndifferent domains. We define a new complex\nand cross-domain semantic parsing and text\u0002to-SQL task where different complex SQL\nqueries and databases appear in train and test\nsets. In this way, the task requires the model\nto generalize well to both new SQL queries\nand new database schemas. Spider is distinct\nfrom most of the previous semantic parsing\ntasks because they all use a single database\nand the exact same programs in the train set\nand the test set. We experiment with vari\u0002ous state-of-the-art models and the best model\nachieves only 12.4% exact matching accu\u0002racy on a database split setting. This shows\nthat Spider presents a strong challenge for fu\u0002ture research. Our dataset and task are pub\u0002licly available at https://yale-lily.\ngithub.io/spider.\n1 Introduction\nSemantic parsing (SP) is one of the most important\ntasks in natural language processing (NLP). It re\u0002quires both understanding the meaning of natural\nlanguage sentences and mapping them to mean\u0002ingful executable queries such as logical forms,\nSQL queries, and Python code.\nRecently, some state-of-the-art methods with\nSeq2Seq architectures are able to achieve over\n80% exact matching accuracy even on some com\u0002plex benchmarks such as ATIS and GeoQuery.\nThese models seem to have already solved most\nproblems in this field.\nHowever, previous tasks in this field have a sim\u0002ple but problematic task definition because most of\nthese results are predicted by semantic “matching”\nDatabase Collection and Creation\n200 databases (DB)\n150 man-hours\nQuestion and SQL Annotation\n20-50 examples per DB\n500 man-hours\nSQL Review\n150 man-hours\nQuestion Review and Paraphrase\n150 man-hours\nFinal Review and Processing\n150 man-hours\nID department_code course_description course_credit\ninstructor\nTable name Columns\nTable 1\ncourse_code department_code course_description course_credit\nTable 2 department\nWhat are the name and budget of the departments\nwith average instructor salary greater than the\noverall average?\nSELECT T1.department_name, T2.budget\nFROM instructor as T1 JOIN department as\nT2 ON T1.department_name = T2.name \nGROUP BY T1.department_name\nHAVING avg(T1.salary) \u003e \n     (SELECT avg(salary) FROM instructor)\nComplex\nquestion\nComplex\nSQL\nAnnotators create:\nID name department_name salary ....\nname building budget .......\nprimary key\nforeign key\nAnnotators check database schema    (e.g., database: college) ......\nTable n\nabase Collection\n\u0026 Creation\n databases (DB)\n50 man-hours\nQuestion and SQL\nAnnotation\n20-50 examples per DB\n500 man-hours\neview\nn-hours\nQuestion Review\n\u0026 Paraphrase\n150 man-hours\nFinal Review \u0026\nProcessing\n150 man-hours\ninstructor\nTable name Columns\nTable 1\nTable 2 department\nWhat are the name and budget of the departments\nwith average instructor salary greater than the\noverall average?\nSELECT T2.name, T2.budget\nFROM instructor as T1 JOIN department as\nT2 ON T1.department_id = T2.id \nGROUP BY T1.department_id\nHAVING avg(T1.salary) \u003e \n    (SELECT avg(salary) FROM instructor)\nComplex\nquestion\nComplex\nSQL\nAnnotators create:\nid name department_id salary ....\nid name building budget .......\nprimary\nforeign key\nAnnotators check database schema   (e.g., database: college) ......\nTable n\nkey\nFigure 1: Our corpus annotates complex questions and\nSQLs. The example contains joining of multiple tables,\na GROUP BY component, and a nested query.\nrather than semantic parsing. Existing datasets for\nSP have two shortcomings. First, those that have\ncomplex programs (Zelle and Mooney, 1996; Li\nand Jagadish, 2014; Yaghmazadeh et al., 2017a;\nIyer et al., 2017) are too small in terms of the num\u0002ber of programs for training modern data-intensive\nmodels and have only a single dataset, meaning\nthat the same database is used for both training\nand testing the model. More importantly, the num\u0002ber of logic forms or SQL labels is small and\neach program has about 4-10 paraphrases of nat\u0002ural language problem to expand the size of the\ndataset. Therefore, the exact same target programs\nappear in both the train and test sets. The mod\u0002els can achieve decent performances even on very\ncomplex programs by memorizing the patterns of\nquestion and program pairs during training and de\u0002coding the programs exactly the same way as it\nsaw in the training set during testing. Finegan\u0002Dollak et al. (2018) split the dataset by programs\nso that no two identical programs would be in both\narXiv:1809.08887v5 [cs.CL] 2 Feb 2019\nthe train and test sets. They show that the models\nbuilt on this question-splitting data setting fail to\ngeneralize to unseen programs. Second, existing\ndatasets that are large in terms of the number of\nprograms and databases such as WikiSQL (Zhong\net al., 2017) contain only simple SQL queries and\nsingle tables. In order to test a model’s real se\u0002mantic parsing performance on unseen complex\nprograms and its ability to generalize to new do\u0002mains, an SP dataset that includes a large amount\nof complex programs and databases with multiple\ntables is a must.\nHowever, compared to other large, realistic\ndatasets such as ImageNet for object recognition\n(Deng et al., 2009) and SQuAD for reading com\u0002prehension (Rajpurkar et al., 2016), creating such\nSP dataset is even more time-consuming and chal\u0002lenging in some aspects due to the following rea\u0002sons. First, it is hard to find many databases\nwith multiple tables online. Second, given a\ndatabase, annotators have to understand the com\u0002plex database schema to create a set of questions\nsuch that their corresponding SQL queries cover\nall SQL patterns. Moreover, it is even more chal\u0002lenging to write different complex SQL queries.\nAdditionally, reviewing and quality-checking of\nquestion and SQL pairs takes a significant amount\nof time. All of these processes require very spe\u0002cific knowledge in databases.\nTo address the need for a large and high-quality\ndataset for a new complex and cross-domain se\u0002mantic parsing task, we introduce Spider, which\nconsists of 200 databases with multiple tables,\n10,181 questions, and 5,693 corresponding com\u0002plex SQL queries, all written by 11 college stu\u0002dents spending a total of 1,000 man-hours. As\nFigure 1 illustrates, given a database with multiple\ntables including foreign keys, our corpus creates\nand annotates complex questions and SQL queries\nincluding different SQL clauses such as joining\nand nested query. In order to generate the SQL\nquery given the input question, models need to un\u0002derstand both the natural language question and\nrelationships between tables and columns in the\ndatabase schema.\nIn addition, we also propose a new task for\nthe text-to-SQL problem. Since Spider contains\n200 databases with foreign keys, we can split the\ndataset with complex SQL queries in a way that\nno database overlaps in train and test, which over\u0002comes the two shortcomings of prior datasets, and\ndefines a new semantic parsing task in which the\nmodel needs to generalize not only to new pro\u0002grams but also to new databases. Models have to\ntake questions and database schemas as inputs and\npredict unseen queries on new databases.\nTo assess the task difficulty, we experiment with\nseveral state-of-the-art semantic parsing models.\nAll of them struggle with this task. The best model\nachieves only 12.4% exact matching accuracy in\nthe database split setting. This suggests that there\nis a large room for improvement.\n2 Related Work and Existing Datasets\nSeveral semantic parsing datasets with different\nqueries have been created. The output can be in\nmany formats, e.g., logic forms. These datasets\ninclude ATIS (Price, 1990; Dahl et al., 1994), Geo\u0002Query (Zelle and Mooney, 1996), and JOBS (Tang\nand Mooney, 2001a). They have been studied ex\u0002tensively (Zelle and Mooney, 1996; Zettlemoyer\nand Collins, 2005; Wong and Mooney, 2007; Das\net al., 2010; Liang et al., 2011; Banarescu et al.,\n2013; Artzi and Zettlemoyer, 2013; Reddy et al.,\n2014; Berant and Liang, 2014; Dong and Lapata,\n2016). However, they are domain specific and\nthere is no standard label guidance for multiple\nSQL queries.\nRecently, more semantic parsing datasets using\nSQL as programs have been created. Iyer et al.\n(2017) and Popescu et al. (2003a) labeled SQL\nqueries for ATIS and GeoQuery datasets. Other\nexisting text-to-SQL datasets also include Restau\u0002rants (Tang and Mooney, 2001b; Popescu et al.,\n2003a), Scholar (Iyer et al., 2017), Academic\n(Li and Jagadish, 2014), Yelp and IMDB (Yagh\u0002mazadeh et al., 2017b), Advising (Finegan-Dollak\net al., 2018), and WikiSQL (Zhong et al., 2017).\nThese datasets have been studied for decades in\nboth the NLP community (Warren and Pereira,\n1982; Popescu et al., 2003b, 2004; Li et al., 2006;\nGiordani and Moschitti, 2012; Wang et al., 2017;\nIyer et al., 2017; Zhong et al., 2017; Xu et al.,\n2017; Yu et al., 2018; Huang et al., 2018; Wang\net al., 2018; Dong and Lapata, 2018; McCann\net al., 2018) and the Database community (Li and\nJagadish, 2014; Yaghmazadeh et al., 2017b). We\nprovide detailed statistics on these datasets in Ta\u0002ble 1.\nMost of the previous work train their models\nwithout schemas as inputs because they use a sin\u0002gle database for both training and testing. Thus,\nthey do not need to generalize to new domains.\nMost importantly, these datasets have a limited\nnumber of labeled logic forms or SQL queries.\nIn order to expand the size of these datasets and\napply neural network approaches, each logic form\nor SQL query has about 4-10 paraphrases for the\nnatural language input. Most previous studies fol\u0002low the standard question-based train and test split\n(Zettlemoyer and Collins, 2005). This way, the ex\u0002act same target queries (with similar paraphrases)\nin the test appear in training set as well. Utiliz\u0002ing this assumption, existing models can achieve\ndecent performances even on complex programs\nby memorizing database-specific SQL templates.\nHowever, this accuracy is artificially inflated be\u0002cause the model merely needs to decide which\ntemplate to use during testing. Finegan-Dollak\net al. (2018) show that template-based approaches\ncan get even higher results. To avoid getting this\ninflated result, Finegan-Dollak et al. (2018) pro\u0002pose a new, program-based splitting evaluation,\nwhere the exact same queries do not appear in\nboth training and testing. They show that un\u0002der this framework, the performance of all the\ncurrent state-of-the-art semantic parsing systems\ndrops dramatically even on the same database, in\u0002dicating that these models fail to generalize to un\u0002seen queries. This indicates that current studies in\nsemantic parsing have limitations.\nWe also want the model to generalize not only\nto unseen queries but also to unseen databases.\nZhong et al. (2017) published the WikiSQL\ndataset. In their problem definition, the databases\nin the test set do not appear in the train or devel\u0002opment sets. Also, the task needs to take differ\u0002ent table schemas as inputs. Therefore, the model\nhas to generalize to new databases. However, in\norder to generate 80654 questions and SQL pairs\nfor 24241 databases, Zhong et al. (2017) made\nsimplified assumptions about the SQL queries and\ndatabases. Their SQL labels only cover single\nSELECT column and aggregation, and WHERE\nconditions. Moreover, all the databases only con\u0002tain single tables. No JOIN, GROUP BY, and\nORDER BY, etc. are included.\nRecently, researchers have constructed some\ndatasets for code generation including IFTTT\n(Quirk et al., 2015), DJANGO (Oda et al., 2015),\nHEARTHSTONE (Ling et al., 2016), NL2Bash\n(Lin et al., 2018), and CoNaLa (Yin et al., 2018).\nThese tasks parse natural language descriptions\nSQL Review\n150 man-hours\nQuestion Review and Paraphrase\n150 man-hours\nFinal Review a\n150 ma\nDatabase Collection\n\u0026 Creation\n200 databases (DB)\n150 man-hours\nQuestion and SQL\nAnnotation\n20-50 examples per DB\n500 man-hours\nSQL Review\n150 man-hours\nQuestion Review\n\u0026 Paraphrase\n150 man-hours\nFinal Review \u0026\nProcessing\n150 man-hours\nFigure 2: The annotation process of our Spider corpus.\ninto a more general-purpose programming lan\u0002guage such as Python (Allamanis et al., 2015; Ling\net al., 2016; Rabinovich et al., 2017; Yin and Neu\u0002big, 2017).\n3 Corpus Construction\nAll questions and SQL queries were written and\nreviewed by 11 computer science students. Some\nof them were native English speakers. As illus\u0002trated in Figure 2, we develop our dataset in five\nsteps, spending around 1,000 hours of human la\u0002bor in total: §3.1 Database Collection and Cre\u0002ation, §3.2 Question and SQL Annotation, §3.3\nSQL Review, §3.4 Question Review and Para\u0002phrase, §3.5 Final Question and SQL Review.\n3.1 Database Collection and Creation\nCollecting databases with complex schemas is\nhard. Although relational databases are widely\nused in industry and academia, most of them are\nnot publicly available. Only a few databases with\nmultiple tables are easily accessible online.\nOur 200 databases covering 138 different do\u0002mains are collected from three resources. First,\nwe collected about 70 complex databases from dif\u0002ferent college database courses, SQL tutorial web\u0002sites, online csv files, and textbook examples. Sec\u0002ond, we collected about 40 databases from the\nDatabaseAnswers1 where contains over 1,000 data\nmodels across different domains. These data mod\u0002els contain only database schemas. We converted\nthem into SQLite, populated them using an on\u0002line database population tool2\n, and then manu\u0002ally corrected some important fields so that the ta\u0002ble contents looked natural. Finally, we created\nthe remaining 90 databases based on WikiSQL.\nTo ensure the domain diversity, we select about\n500 tables in about 90 different domains to cre\u0002ate these 90 databases. To create each database,\nwe chose several related tables from WikiSQL\n1http://www.databaseanswers.org/\n2http://filldb.info/\ndev or test splits, and then created a relational\ndatabase schema with foreign keys based on the\ntables we selected. We had to create some inter\u0002section tables in order to link several tables to\u0002gether. For most other cases, we did not need to\npopulate these databases since tables in WikiSQL\nare from Wikipedia, which already had real world\ndata stored.\nWe manually corrected some database schemas\nif they had some column names that did not make\nsense or missed some foreign keys. For table and\ncolumn names, it is common to use abbreviations\nin databases. For example, ‘student id’ might be\nrepresented by ‘stu id’. For our task definition, we\nmanually changed each column name back to reg\u0002ular words so that the system only handled seman\u0002tic parsing issues.\n3.2 Question and SQL Annotation\nFor each database, we ask eight computer science\nstudents proficient in SQL to create 20-50 natu\u0002ral questions and their SQL labels. To make our\nquestions diverse, natural, and reflective of how\nhumans actually use databases, we did not use any\ntemplate or script to generate question and SQL\nqueries. Our annotation procedure ensures the fol\u0002lowing three aspects.\nA) SQL pattern coverage. We ensure that\nour corpus contains enough examples for all\ncommon SQL patterns. For each database, we\nask annotators to write SQL queries that cover\nall the following SQL components: SELECT\nwith multiple columns and aggregations, WHERE,\nGROUP BY, HAVING, ORDER BY, LIMIT,\nJOIN, INTERSECT, EXCEPT, UNION, NOT\nIN, OR, AND, EXISTS, LIKE as well as nested\nqueries. The annotators made sure that each table\nin the database appears in at least one query.\nB) SQL consistency. Some questions have mul\u0002tiple acceptable SQL queries with the same re\u0002sult. However, giving totally different SQL labels\nto similar questions can hinder the training of se\u0002mantic parsing models. To avoid this issue, we\ndesigned the annotation protocol so that all anno\u0002tators choose the same SQL query pattern if mul\u0002tiple equivalent queries are possible.\nC) Question clarity. We did not create ques\u0002tions that are (1) vague or too ambiguous, or (2)\nrequire knowledge outside the database to answer.\nFirst, ambiguous questions refer to the ques\u0002tions that do not have enough clues to infer which\ncolumns to return and which conditions to con\u0002sider. For example, we would not ask “What is\nthe most popular class at University X?” because\nthe definition of “popular” is not clear: it could\nmean the rating of the class or the number of stu\u0002dents taking the course. Instead, we choose to ask\n“What is the name of the class which the largest\nnumber of students are taking at University X?”.\nHere, “popular” refers to the size of student en\u0002rollment. Thus, the “student enrollment” column\ncan be used in condition to answer this question.\nWe recognize that ambiguous questions appear in\nreal-world natural language database interfaces.\nWe agree that future work needs to address this\nissue by having multi-turn interactions between\nthe system and users for clarification. However,\nour main aim here is to develop a corpus to tackle\nthe problem of handling complex queries and gen\u0002eralizing across databases without multi-turn in\u0002teractions required, which no existing semantic\nparsing datasets could do. Moreover, the low per\u0002formances of current state-of-the-art models al\u0002ready show that our task is challenging enough,\nwithout ambiguous questions. In addition, ques\u0002tions are required to contain the specific informa\u0002tion to return. Otherwise, we don’t know if class\nid is also acceptable in the previous case. Most\nof the questions in the existing semantic parsing\ndatasets are ambiguous. This is not a serious prob\u0002lem if we use one single dataset because we have\nenough data domain specific examples to know\nwhich columns are the default. However, it would\nbe a serious problem in cross domain tasks since\nthe default return values differ cross domain and\npeople.\nSecond, humans sometimes ask questions that\nrequire common sense knowledge outside the\ngiven database. For instance, when people ask\n“Display the employee id for the employees who\nreport to John”, the correct SQL is\nSELECT employee id\nFROM employees\nWHERE manager id = (\nSELECT employee id\nFROM employees\nWHERE first name = ‘John’)\nwhich requires the common knowledge that “X\nreports to Y” corresponds to an “employee-\nmanager” relation. we do not include such ques\u0002tions and leave them as a future research direction.\nAnnotation tools We open each database on a\nweb-based interface powered by the sqlite web3\ntool. It allows the annotators to see the schema\nand content of each table, execute SQL queries,\nand check the returned results. This tool was ex\u0002tremely helpful for the annotators to write exe\u0002cutable SQL queries that reflect the true meaning\nof the given questions and return correct answers.\n3.3 SQL Review\nOnce the database is labeled with question-query\npairs, we ask a different annotator to check if the\nquestions are clear and contain enough informa\u0002tion to answer the query. For a question with\nmultiple possible SQL translations, the reviewers\ndouble check whether the SQL label is correctly\nchosen under our protocol. Finally, the reviewers\ncheck if all the SQL labels in the current database\ncover all the common SQL clauses.\n3.4 Question Review and Paraphrase\nAfter SQL labels are reviewed, native English\nspeakers review and correct each question. They\nfirst check if the question is grammatically correct\nand natural. Next, they make sure that the question\nreflects the meaning of its corresponding SQL la\u0002bel. Finally, to improve the diversity in questions,\nwe ask annotators to add a paraphrased version to\nsome questions.\n3.5 Final Review\nFinally, we ask the most experienced annotator to\nconduct the final question and SQL review. This\nannotator makes the final decision if multiple re\u0002viewers are not sure about some annotation issues.\nAlso, we run a script to execute and parse all SQL\nlabels to make sure they are correct.\n4 Dataset Statistics and Comparison\nWe summarize the statistics of Spider and other\ntext-to-SQL datasets in Table 1. Compared with\nother datasets, Spider contains databases with\nmultiple tables and contains SQL queries in\u0002cluding many complex SQL components. For\nexample, Spider contains about twice more\nnested queries and 10 times more ORDER BY\n3https://github.com/coleifer/\nsqlite-web\n(LIMIT) and GROUP BY (HAVING) compo\u0002nents than the total of previous text-to-SQL\ndatasets. Spider has 200 distinct databases cov\u0002ering 138 different domains such as college, club,\nTV show, government, etc. Most domains have\none database, thus containing 20-50 questions, and\na few domains such as flight information have\nmultiple databases with more than 100 questions\nin total. On average, each database in Spider\nhas 27.6 columns and 8.8 foreign keys. The av\u0002erage question length and SQL length are about\n13 and 21 respectively. Our task uses different\ndatabases for training and testing, evaluating the\ncross-domain performance. Therefore, Spider is\nthe only one text-to-SQL dataset that contains both\ndatabases with multiple tables in different domains\nand complex SQL queries It tests the ability of a\nsystem to generalize to not only new SQL queries\nand database schemas but also new domains.\n5 Task Definition\nOn top of the proposed dataset, we define a text\u0002to-SQL task that is more realistic than prior work.\nUnlike most of the previous semantic parsing or\ntext-to-SQL tasks, models will be tested on both\ndifferent complex SQL queries and different com\u0002plex databases in different domains in our task. It\naims to ensure that models can only make the cor\u0002rect prediction when they truly understand the se\u0002mantic meaning of the questions, rather than just\nmemorization. Also, because our databases con\u0002tain different domains, our corpus tests model’s\nability to generalize to new databases. In this way,\nmodel performance on this task can reflect the real\nsemantic parsing ability.\nIn order to make the task feasible and to focus\non the more fundamental part of semantic parsing,\nwe make the following assumptions:\n• In our current task, we do not evaluate model\nperformance on generating values. Predicting\ncorrect SQL structures and columns is more re\u0002alistic and critical at this stage based on the\nlow performances of various current state-of\u0002the-art models on our task. In a real world situ\u0002ation, people need to double check what condi\u0002tion values are and finalize them after multiple\ntimes. It is unrealistic to predict condition val\u0002ues without interacting with users. In reality,\nmost people know what values to ask but do not\nknow the SQL logic. A more reasonable way is\nto ask users to use an interface searching the\nDataset # Q # SQL # DB # Domain # Table / DB ORDER BY GROUP BY NESTED HAVING\nATIS 5,280 947 1 1 32 0 5 315 0\nGeoQuery 877 247 1 1 6 20 46 167 9\nScholar 817 193 1 1 7 75 100 7 20\nAcademic 196 185 1 1 15 23 40 7 18\nIMDB 131 89 1 1 16 10 6 1 0\nYelp 128 110 1 1 7 18 21 0 4\nAdvising 3,898 208 1 1 10 15 9 22 0\nRestaurants 378 378 1 1 3 0 0 4 0\nWikiSQL 80,654 77,840 26,521 - 1 0 0 0 0\nSpider 10,181 5,693 200 138 5.1 1335 1491 844 388\nTable 1: Comparisons of text-to-SQL datasets. Spider is the only one text-to-SQL dataset that contains both\ndatabases with multiple tables in different domains and complex SQL queries. It was designed to test the ability of\na system to generalize to not only new SQL queries and database schemas but also new domains.\nvalues, then ask more specific questions. Also,\nother previous work with value prediction uses\none single database in both train and test which\nmakes it vulnerable to overfitting. However,\nSQL queries must include values in order to ex\u0002ecute them. For value prediction in our task, a\nlist of gold values for each question is given.\nModels need to fill them into the right slots in\ntheir predicted SQL.\n• As mentioned in the previous sections, we ex\u0002clude some queries that require outside knowl\u0002edge such as common sense inference and\nmath calculation. For example, imagine a ta\u0002ble with birth and death year columns. To\nanswer the questions like “How long is X’s\nlife length?”, we use SELECT death year\n- birth year. Even though this example\nis easy for humans, it requires some common\nknowledge of the life length definition and the\nuse of a math operation, which is not the focus\nof our dataset.\n• We assume all table and column names in the\ndatabase are clear and self-contained. For ex\u0002ample, some databases use database specific\nshort-cut names for table and column names\nsuch as “stu id”, which we manually converted\nto “student id” in our corpus.\n6 Evaluation Metrics\nOur evaluation metrics include Component\nMatching, Exact Matching, and Execution Ac\u0002curacy. In addition, we measure the system’s\naccuracy as a function of the difficulty of a query.\nSince our task definition does not predict value\nstring, our evaluation metrics do not take value\nstrings into account.\nWe will release the official evaluation script\nalong with our corpus so that the research com\u0002munity can share the same evaluation platform.\nComponent Matching To conduct a detailed\nanalysis of model performance, we measure the\naverage exact match between the prediction and\nground truth on different SQL components. For\neach of the following components:\n• SELECT • WHERE • GROUP BY\n• ORDER BY • KEYWORDS (including all\nSQL keywords without column names and\noperators)\nwe decompose each component in the prediction\nand the ground truth as bags of several sub\u0002components, and check whether or not these two\nsets of components match exactly. To evaluate\neach SELECT component, for example, con\u0002sider SELECT avg(col1), max(col2),\nmin(col1), we first parse and decompose into\na set (avg, min, col1), (max, col2),\nand see if the gold and predicted sets are the same.\nPrevious work directly compared decoded SQL\nwith gold SQL. However, some SQL components\ndo not have order constraints. In our evaluation,\nwe treat each component as a set so that for ex\u0002ample, SELECT avg(col1), min(col1),\nmax(col2) and SELECT avg(col1),\nmax(col2), min(col1) would be treated\nas the same query. To report a model’s overall\nperformance on each component, we compute F1\nscore on exact set matching.\nExact Matching We measure whether the pre\u0002dicted query as a whole is equivalent to the gold\nquery. We first evaluate the SQL clauses as de\u0002scribed in the last section. The predicted query\nis correct only if all of the components are cor\u0002rect. Because we conduct a set comparison in each\nclause, this exact matching metric can handle the\n“ordering issue” (Xu et al., 2017).\nExecution Accuracy4 Since Exact Matching is\npossible to provide false negative evaluation when\nthe semantic parser is able to generate novel syn\u0002tax structures, we also consider Execution Accu\u0002racy. For Execution Accuracy, the value is a must\nin order to execute SQL queries. Instead of gener\u0002ating these values, a list of gold values for each\nquestion is given. Models need to select them\nand fill them into the right slots in their predicted\nSQL. We exclude value prediction in Component\nand Exact Matching evaluations and do not pro\u0002vide Execution Accuracy in the current version.\nHowever, it is also important to note that Execu\u0002tion Accuracy can create false positive evaluation\nwhen a predicted SQL returns the same result (for\nexample, ‘NULL’) as the gold SQL while they are\nsemantically different. So we can use both to com\u0002plement each other.\nFinally, our evaluation also considers multiple\nacceptable keys if JOIN and GROUP are in the\nquery. For example, suppose “stu id” in one ta\u0002ble refers to “stu id” in another table, GROUP BY\neither is acceptable.\nSQL Hardness Criteria To better understand\nthe model performance on different queries, we\ndivide SQL queries into 4 levels: easy, medium,\nhard, extra hard. We define the difficulty\nbased on the number of SQL components, selec\u0002tions, and conditions, so that queries that contain\nmore SQL keywords (GROUP BY, ORDER BY,\nINTERSECT, nested subqueries, column selec\u0002tions and aggregators, etc) are considered to be\nharder. For example, a query is considered as hard\nif it includes more than two SELECT columns,\nmore than two WHERE conditions, and GROUP\nBY two columns, or contains EXCEPT or nested\nqueries. A SQL with more additions on top of that\nis considered as extra hard. Figure 3 shows exam\u0002ples of SQL queries in 4 hardness levels.\n7 Methods\nIn order to analyze the difficulty and demonstrate\nthe purpose of our corpus, we experiment with\nseveral state-of-the-art semantic parsing models.\nAs our dataset is fundamentally different from the\nprior datasets such as Geoquery and WikiSQL,\nwe adapted these models to our task as follows.\nWe created a ‘big’ column list by concatenating\ncolumns in all tables of the database together as\n4\nPlease check our website for the latest updates on the\ntask at https://yale-lily.github.io/spider\nWhat is the number of cars with more than 4 cylinders?\nSELECT COUNT(*)\nFROM cars_data\nWHERE cylinders \u003e 4\nFor each stadium, how many concerts are there?\nSELECT T2.name, COUNT(*)\nFROM concert AS T1 JOIN stadium AS T2\nON T1.stadium_id = T2.stadium_id\nGROUP BY T1.stadium_id\nWhich countries in Europe have at least 3 car\nmanufacturers?\nSELECT T1.country_name\nFROM countries AS T1 JOIN continents\nAS T2 ON T1.continent = T2.cont_id\nJOIN car_makers AS T3 ON\nT1.country_id = T3.country\nWHERE T2.continent = 'Europe'\nGROUP BY T1.country_name\nHAVING COUNT(*) \u003e= 3\nWhat is the average life expectancy in the countries\nwhere English is not the official language?\nSELECT AVG(life_expectancy)\nFROM country\nWHERE name NOT IN \n   (SELECT T1.name\n    FROM country AS T1 JOIN\n    country_language AS T2\n    ON T1.code = T2.country_code\n    WHERE T2.language = \"English\"\n      AND T2.is_official = \"T\")\nEasy\nMeidum\nHard\nExtra Hard\nFigure 3: SQL query examples in 4 hardness levels.\nan input to all models. Also, for each model, we\nlimit the column selection space for each question\nexample to all column of the database which the\nquestion is asking instead of all column names in\nthe whole corpus.\nSeq2Seq Inspired by neural machine translation\n(Sutskever et al., 2014), we first apply a basic\nsequence-to-sequence model, Seq2Seq. Then, we\nalso explore Seq2Seq+Attention from (Dong and\nLapata, 2016) by adding an attention mechanism\n(Bahdanau et al., 2015). In addition, we include\nSeq2Seq+Copying by adding an attention-based\nTest Dev\nEasy Medium Hard Extra Hard All All\nExample Split\nSeq2Seq 22.0 7.8 5.5 1.3 9.4 10.3\nSeq2Seq+Attention (Dong and Lapata, 2016) 32.3 15.6 10.3 2.3 15.9 16.0\nSeq2Seq+Copying 29.3 13.1 8.8 3.0 14.1 15.3\nSQLNet (Xu et al., 2017) 34.1 19.6 11.7 3.3 18.3 18.4\nTypeSQL (Yu et al., 2018) 47.5 38.4 24.1 14.4 33.0 34.4\nDatabase Split\nSeq2Seq 11.9 1.9 1.3 0.5 3.7 1.9\nSeq2Seq+Attention (Dong and Lapata, 2016) 14.9 2.5 2.0 1.1 4.8 1.8\nSeq2Seq+Copying 15.4 3.4 2.0 1.1 5.3 4.1\nSQLNet (Xu et al., 2017) 26.2 12.6 6.6 1.3 12.4 10.9\nTypeSQL (Yu et al., 2018) 19.6 7.6 3.8 0.8 8.2 8.0\nTable 2: Accuracy of Exact Matching on SQL queries with different hardness levels.\nMethod SELECT WHERE GROUP BY ORDER BY KEYWORDS\nExample Split\nSeq2Seq 23.3 4.9 15.3 9.2 17.9\nSeq2Seq+Attention 31.1 9.1 28.2 20.8 21.4\nSeq2Seq+Copying 28.2 8.3 25.5 21.3 19.0\nSQLNet 59.8 32.9 35.9 65.5 76.1\nTypeSQL 77.3 52.4 47.0 67.5 78.4\nDatabase Split\nSeq2Seq 13.0 1.5 3.3 5.3 8.7\nSeq2Seq+Attention 13.6 3.1 3.6 9.9 9.9\nSeq2Seq+Copying 12.0 3.1 5.3 5.8 7.3\nSQLNet 44.5 19.8 29.5 48.8 64.0\nTypeSQL 36.4 16.0 17.2 47.7 66.2\nTable 3: F1 scores of Component Matching on all SQL queries on Test set.\ncopying operation similar to (Jia and Liang, 2016).\nThe original model does not take the schema\ninto account because it has the same schema in\nboth train and test. We modify the model so that it\nconsiders the table schema information by passing\na vocabulary mask that limits the model to decode\nthe words from SQL keywords, table and column\nnames in the current database.\nSQLNet introduced by (Xu et al., 2017) uses\ncolumn attention and employs a sketch-based\nmethod and generates SQL as a slot-filling\ntask. This fundamentally avoids the sequence-to\u0002sequence structure when ordering does not matter\nin SQL query conditions. Because it is originally\ndesigned for WikiSQL, we extend its SELECT and\nWHERE modules to ORDER BY and GROUP BY\ncomponents.\nTypeSQL proposed by (Yu et al., 2018) im\u0002proves upon SQLNet by proposing a different\ntraining procedure and utilizing types extracted\nfrom either knowledge graph or table content to\nhelp model better understand entities and numbers\nin the question. In our experiment, we use the\nquestion type info extracted from database con\u0002tent. Also, we extend their modules to ORDER BY\nand GROUP BY components as well. It is the only\nmodel that uses database content.\n8 Experimental Results and Discussion\nWe summarize the performance of all models on\nour test set including accuracy of exact matching\nin Table 2 and F1 scores of component matching\nin Table 3.\nData Splits For the final training dataset, we\nalso select and include 752 queries and 1659 ques\u0002tions that follow our annotation protocol from six\nexisting datasets: Restaurants, GeoQuery, Scholar,\nAcademic, IMDB, and Yelp. We report results on\ntwo different settings for all models: (1) Exam\u0002ple split where examples are randomly split into\n8659 train, 1034 dev, 2147 test. Questions for the\nsame database can appear in both train and test.\n(2) Database split where 206 databases are split\ninto 146 train, 20 dev, and 40 test. All questions\nfor the same database are in the same split.\nOverall Performance The performances of the\nSeq2Seq-based basic models including Seq2Seq,\nSeq2Seq+Attention, and Seq2Seq+Copying are\nvery low. However, they are able to generate\nnested and complex queries because of their gen\u0002eral decoding process. Thus, they can get a few\nhard and extra hard examples correct. But in the\nvast majority of cases, they predict invalid SQL\nqueries with grammatical errors. The attention and\ncopying mechanisms do not help much either.\nIn contrast, SQLNet and TypeSQL that uti\u0002lize SQL structure information to guide the SQL\ngeneration process significantly outperform other\nSeq2Seq models. While they can produce valid\nqueries, however, they are unable to generate\nnested queries or queries with keywords such as\nEXCEPT and INTERSECT because they limit\npossible SQL outputs in some fixed pre-defined\nSQL structures.\nAs Component Matching results in Table 3\nshows, all models struggle with WHERE clause\nprediction the most. WHERE clause is more likely\nto have multiple columns and operations, which\nmakes its prediction the most challenging. The\nmost number of prediction errors for each com\u0002ponent is from column prediction.\nIn general, the overall performances of all mod\u0002els are low, indicating that our task is challenging\nand there is still a large room for improvement.\nExample Split vs Database Split As discussed\nin Section 5, another challenge of the dataset is to\ngeneralize to new databases. To study this, in Ta\u0002ble 2 and Table 3 we compare model performances\nunder the two settings. For all models, the per\u0002formance under database split is much lower than\nthat under example split. Especially, TypeSQL uti\u0002lizes column names as question types, and it out\u0002performs other models with a large margin under\nthe example split. However, its performance drops\nthe most on the database split data set. This in\u0002dicates that the model does well on complex SQL\nprediction but fails to generalize to new databases.\nIn addition, we observe that all models perform\nmuch poorer on column selection under database\nsplit than example split.\nOverall, the result shows that our dataset\npresents a challenge for the model to generalize\nto new databases.\nFigure 4: Exact matching accuracy as a function of the\nnumber of foreign keys.\nComplexity of Database Schema In order to\nshow how the complexity of the database schema\naffects model performance, Figure 4 plots the ex\u0002act matching accuracy as a function of the number\nof foreign keys in a database. The performance\ndecreases as the database has more foreign keys.\nThe first reason is that the model has to choose\ncolumn and table names from many candidates in\na complex database schema. Second, a complex\ndatabase schema presents a great challenge for the\nmodel to capture the relationship between differ\u0002ent tables with foreign keys. SQL answers to ques\u0002tions on the database with more number of foreign\nkeys are more likely to join more tables. It indi\u0002cates that this task requires more effective meth\u0002ods to encode the relation of tables with foreign\nkeys.\n9 Conclusion\nIn this paper we introduce Spider, a large, com\u0002plex and cross-domain semantic parsing and text\u0002to-SQL dataset, which directly benefits both NLP\nand DB communities. Based on Spider, we define\na new challenging and realistic semantic parsing\ntask. Result:

```json
{
  "markdown": "Spider\n\n# Spider\n\n## Introduction\n\nSpider is a large-scale, complex and cross-domain semantic parsing and Text2SQL dataset annotated by 11 college students. It consists of 10,181 questions and 5,693 unique complex SQL queries on 200 databases with multiple tables, covering 138 different domains.\n\nThe goal of Spider is converting the textual representation to an SQL query.\n\n## Assumptions \u0026 Guarantees\n\nTo create Spider, some assumptions on the dataset were made:\n\n- SQL Coverage: The dataset should cover all practical SQL queries\n- SQL Consistency: The annotation protocol is deterministic. All annotators will create the same SQL query given a question.\n- Question Clarity: For every question, there is exactly one valid query (up to semantic equivalence). That is, one should be able to infer the query from the question without resulting to any additional knowledge.\n\nAlso, to avoid overfitting over a particular database. It is guaranteed that training, dev, and test do not share a database.\n\n## SQL Hardness Criteria\n\nTo better understand the model performance on different queries, we divide SQL queries into 4 levels: easy, medium, hard, extra hard. We define the difficulty as the following:\n\nFirst, we define:\n\n- SQL components 1:&#96;WHERE, GROUP BY, ORDER BY, LIMIT, JOIN, OR, LIKE，HAVING&#96;\n- SQL components 2: EXCEPT, UNION, INTERSECT, NESTED\n- Others: number of agg \u003e 1, number of select columns \u003e 1, number of where conditions \u003e 1, number of group by clauses \u003e 1, number of group by clauses \u003e 1 (no consider col1-col2 math equations etc.)\n\nThen different hardness levels are determined as follows.\n\nMedium: Either of below:\n\n- SQL satisfies no more than two rules in [Others], and does not have more than one word from [SQL components 1], and no word from [SQL components 2].\n- SQL has exact 2 words from [SQL components 1] and less than 2 rules in [Others], and no word from [SQL components 2]\n\nHard: Either of below:\n\n- SQL satisfies more than two rules in [Others], with no more than 2 key words in [SQL components 1] and no word in [SQL components 2].\n- SQL has 2 \u003c number key words in [SQL components 1] \u003c= 3 and satisfies no more than two rules in [Others] but no word in [SQL components 2].\n- SQL has no more than 1 key word in [SQL components 1] and no rule in [Others], but exact one key word in [SQL components 2].\n\n## Evaluation Metrics\n\nFour main evaluation metrics were used in Spider:\n\n1. All: all SQL-question pairs to be scored.\n2. [Execution Accuracy](https://survey.convergence-ai-lab.com/datasets/evaluation/execution-accuracy.html)\n3. [Test Suites](https://survey.convergence-ai-lab.com/datasets/evaluation/test-suites.html)\n4. Exact Matching\n5. Exact Matching without Values\n\n### Partial Matching without Values\n\nIn order to know model's performance on different SQL components, we provide the detailed scores on each part. Since models in our paper do not predict value string, our Partial and Exact Matching evaluation metrics do not take value strings into account.\n\nFor each SQL, we compute accuracy and F1 scores for all following components:\n\n1. &#96;SELECT COLUMN&#96;: e.g. gold: ([select, col1, none], [select, col2, max]) and predicted: ([select, col1, none], [select, col3, min]) compute accuracy, recall, precision and F1 scores.\n2. &#96;SELECT COLUMN WITHOUT AGG&#96;: e.g. gold: ([select, col1], [select, col2]) and predicted: ([select, col1], [select, col3]) compute accuracy, recall, precision and F1 scores.\n3. &#96;WHERE COLUMN&#96;: ([where, col4, NOT IN, NESTED SQL], [where, col1, \u003e=, novalue], [where, col2, =, novalue])\n4. &#96;WHERE COLUMN WITHOUT OP&#96;: ([where, col1], [where, col4])\n5. &#96;GROUP BY&#96;: ([groupby, col2], [groupby, col5])\n6. &#96;GROUP BY HAVING&#96;: ([groupby, col2, having col1, count, \u003e=])\n7. &#96;ORDER BY&#96;: ([orderby, col1, no agg, desc, no limit], [orderby, *, count, asc, 3])\n8. &#96;AND/OR&#96;: ([where, col1, col2, and], [where, col3, col2, or])\n9. &#96;EXCEPT, UNION, INTERSECT, NESTED SQL&#96;: get the&#96;except/union/intersect/nested&#96; part in all SQLs containing&#96;except/union/intersect/nested&#96;, check if predicted&#96;except/union/intersect/nested&#96; part equals to the gold&#96;except/union/intersect/nested part&#96;.\n10. &#96;SQL KEY WORDS&#96;: for gold and predicted sql, create a set of SQL key words if they are in&#96;[where, group by, having, desc, asc, order by, limit, except, union, intersect, not in, in, or, like]&#96;.\n\n### Exact Matching without Values\n\nIn Spider, SQL matching is done component-wise using the SQL skeletons. If the predicted result gets all SQL parts right, then the score of Exact Matching without Values for this predicted example is 1, otherwise 0.",
  "url": "https://survey.convergence-ai-lab.com/datasets/spider.html"
}
```
User decision: **accepted**
